docs.title: Core Systems
docs.text:
⚡ Brain & Classifier

Message routing, Haiku classifier (~300 actions), Sonnet brain with tool_use loop, multi-action orchestration

🤖 Discord Infrastructure

bot.py structure, cog system, event bus, middleware chain, progress tracking and monitoring

🛡️ Self-Healing & Supervisor

3-layer defense, circuit breaker, cog auto-recovery, watchdog monitoring and crash protection

docs.title: AI & Recruiting
docs.text:
🎯 PRISM Candidate Scoring

1-5 scoring framework (60% tech / 25% env / 15% role), leadership detection, outreach quality gates

🔍 Sourcing & Outreach Pipeline

End-to-end recruiting: company raids, enrichment, PRISM scoring, personalized outreach generation

🎤 Meeting Intelligence & Notes

Automated meeting notes, speaker identification, action item extraction, cross-meeting analysis

docs.title: Agents & Automation
docs.text:
💻 Copilot & Agent Lab

Persistent Claude Code pipe, Agent Lab (Scout + Analyst), auto-recovery and session persistence

🌙 Overnight Engine & Scheduling

Nightly autonomy cycles ($10 cap), dream mode, APScheduler integration, batch processing

📱 Remote Access & Bridge

Mobile bridge, remote control sessions, night mode, cross-device accessibility

docs.title: Infrastructure
docs.text:
📚 Knowledge Base & RAG

ChromaDB vector store, knowledge graph, 4 memory systems, SQLite persistence and search

🔊 TTS & Voice

ElevenLabs TTS, voice rotation, "read this" intercept, audio summaries and streaming

docs.text:

Multi-sport prop analysis, 4-agent swarm evaluator, 70.2% accuracy, daily predictions

🎮 Valorant & Gaming

Valorant API integration, VLR esports scraping, lineup database, competitive game tracking

💰 Finance & Utilities

Stock tracking, Spotify control, smart home integration, package tracking

## Architecture 
graph LR
    A["💬 Discord Message"] --> B["⚡ Classifier<br/>Haiku Routes to ~300 Actions"]
    B --> C{Decision}
    C -->|Fast Path| D["🤖 Cog Handler"]
    C -->|Complex| E["🧠 Brain<br/>Sonnet + Tool Loop"]
    D --> F["📨 Discord Response"]
    E --> G["🔧 Internal Tools<br/>Channel I/O, Copilot"]
    E --> H["🌐 External Tools<br/>Web, KB, MCP"]
    G --> F
    H --> F

    style A fill:#DC2626,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style B fill:#DC2626,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style E fill:#DC2626,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style F fill:#DC2626,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style C fill:#333,stroke:#DC2626,color:#fff
    style D fill:#444,stroke:#666,color:#fff
    style G fill:#333,stroke:#666,color:#fff
    style H fill:#333,stroke:#666,color:#fff
The FORGE pipeline processes every Discord message through a high-speed classifier that routes to either a cog (fast path) or the brain (complex reasoning). The brain operates a tool-use loop for multi-step tasks, accessing internal tools (channel I/O, copilot), research tools (web search, knowledge base), and MCP integrations (browser, Discord, calendar, etc.).

docs.title: Ashby ATS Integration
docs.text:
Ashby API client for candidate management, job board, applications, and interview scheduling.

File: modules/ashby.py | Lines: ~515

docs.title: What It Does
docs.text: Interfaces with Ashby ATS via REST API (Basic auth). Pulls candidates, jobs, applications, and interview schedules. Formats data as Discord embeds with color coding. Used for pipeline visibility, candidate detail lookups, and application status tracking. All endpoints POST to api.ashbyhq.com.

docs.title: Key Functions
docs.text: Function Purpose _get_headers() Build Basic auth headers (API key as username) _api_call(endpoint, payload) Sync POST to Ashby API with error handling _async_api(endpoint, payload) Async wrapper for bot commands get_candidate(candidate_id) Fetch single candidate detail get_pipeline() List all pipeline stages and applications get_applications(filters) Query applications with status filtering

docs.title: Dependencies
docs.text:
• requests (sync), asyncio (async wrapper)
  • discord.py for embed formatting
  • ASHBY_API_KEY from config
  • API Docs: https://developers.ashbyhq.com/reference

docs.title: Autonomy Loop
docs.text:
FORGE's self-improvement engine using 3-model pipeline.

File: core/autonomy_loop.py | Lines: ~350+

docs.title: What It Does
docs.text: FORGE's continuous self-improvement loop. 3-phase pipeline: (1) ANALYZE reads failure logs and capability gaps from local DB, (2) RESEARCH has Haiku investigate root cause (cheap, fast), (3) DESIGN has Opus architect the fix (deep thinking), (4) EXECUTE has Sonnet implement via copilot, (5) TRACK records outcome. Runs every 6 hours, daily budget cap prevents runaway costs. Logs all cycles to autonomy_log.jsonl.

docs.title: Key Components
docs.text: Component Purpose ImprovementCandidate Dataclass for potential improvements (source, title, priority, evidence) ResearchReport Findings from Haiku investigation DesignReport Opus's architecture + risk assessment ExecutionResult Sonnet's implementation outcome

docs.title: Phases
docs.text: Phase Model Task ANALYZE Local Read friction signals, failure logs, capability gaps RESEARCH Haiku Investigate root cause — fast, cheap DESIGN Opus Architecture review, risk assessment — deep EXECUTE Sonnet Implement via copilot — balanced TRACK Local Update state, log outcome

docs.title: Budget & Scheduling
docs.text: Setting Value Schedule Every 6 hours Max improvements/day 5 (env: AUTONOMY_MAX_DAILY) Daily cost cap $5.00 USD (env: AUTONOMY_DAILY_BUDGET) Min confidence 0.5 to auto-improve Design confidence gate 0.4 (must exceed to proceed)

docs.title: Bootstrap
docs.text:
Module initialization sequence for on_ready().

File: core/bootstrap.py | Lines: ~250+

docs.title: What It Does
docs.text: Orchestrates FORGE's startup sequence in dependency order. Extracted from bot.py to reduce file size and enable testability. Each module initializes independently; failures are logged but don't block other modules (except critical ones like database). Phases: core infrastructure, skills, MCPs, cogs, scheduled jobs.

docs.title: Key Functions
docs.text: Function Purpose init_all_modules(bot, prism_active_role) Initialize all modules, return (ok_list, failed_list) _init_monitor() Start FORGE Monitor + memory monitor _init_skills() Load skill manager _init_mcps() Load MCP servers (Letta, Claude², etc.) _init_cogs() Dynamically load all cogs from cogs/ _init_scheduled_jobs() Register APScheduler jobs (autonomy, overnight, etc.)

docs.title: Initialization Phases
docs.text:
1. Core Infrastructure — Monitor, process monitor, database
  2. Skills & MCPs — Skill manager, MCP manager, Letta subconscious
  3. Cogs — All command handlers (34 files)
  4. Scheduled Jobs — Autonomy loop, overnight engine, daily jobs
  5. Event Bus — Cross-system event routing

docs.title: Brain
docs.text:
Claude API wrapper with model switching and token tracking.

File: core/brain.py | Lines: ~300+

docs.title: What It Does
docs.text: Manages all interactions with Anthropic's Claude API. Wraps the Anthropic client, tracks token usage, and provides model switching between Haiku, Sonnet, and Opus. Includes LRU response caching for graceful degradation when API is unavailable.

docs.title: Key Functions
docs.text: Function Purpose __init__() Initialize brain with Anthropic client and set default model ask(prompt, system, model, tools, max_iterations) Send prompt to Claude, handle tool_use loops with retry switch_model(model_name) Switch between haiku, sonnet, opus at runtime get_model_display_name() Get human-readable model name (e.g., "Sonnet") _cache_response(key, response) Store API response in LRU cache (max 100) _get_cached(key) Retrieve cached response if available

docs.title: Classifier Prompt
docs.text:
Intent classification template with 470+ action definitions.

File: core/classifier_prompt.py | Lines: ~2000+

docs.title: What It Does
docs.text: Contains the Haiku classifier's intent definition template. Extracted from bot.py to enable independent versioning. Defines all action types FORGE recognizes (browser, email, calendar, settings, etc.) with examples for each action. Routes natural language messages to the correct handler via JSON classification.

docs.title: Key Actions
docs.text: Action Group Example Actions Browser browser_open, browser_screenshot, browser_tabs, browser_close, browser_read, browser_back Screen screen_capture, screen_describe App app_open, app_close, app_list PC pc_lock, pc_stats, pc_volume, pc_sleep, pc_shutdown, pc_restart Email email_inbox, email_search, email_send, email_reply, email_delete, email_mass_delete Calendar calendar_today, calendar_add, calendar_update, calendar_delete, calendar_reschedule, calendar_freebusy Media media_play_pause, media_next, media_prev, media_volume, media_sort Settings settings_view, settings_update

docs.title: Key Structure
docs.text: Field Purpose _ACTION_CLASSIFY_PROMPT Main classification template with action descriptions HANDLED_ACTIONS Maps action names to handler functions in cogs

docs.title: Company Raider
docs.text:
End-to-end company sourcing pipeline — LinkedIn search, deduplication, and talent extraction.

File: modules/company_raider.py | Lines: ~449

docs.title: What It Does
docs.text: Raids a company for engineering talent. Batches LinkedIn profile searches (2 calls per company: 3-5yr and 6-10yr experience) via RapidAPI, post-filters by location (Bay Area) and experience, deduplicates against Ashby pipeline and candidate cache, and returns qualified candidates ready for PRISM scoring. Stores all profiles with senior_backlog tag for profiles 8+ years.

docs.title: Key Functions
docs.text: Function Purpose raid(company_name, max_profiles) Main entry point — batch search, dedupe, return results _search_linkedin(query, count) RapidAPI call to search for profiles _dedupe_against_pipeline(profiles) Filter profiles already in Ashby _filter_by_location(profiles) Keep Bay Area only _filter_by_experience(profiles) Keep 2-7yr (or tag 8+ for backlog) print_raid_results(results) Format for user display

docs.title: Dependencies
docs.text:
• aiohttp — async HTTP for RapidAPI
  • RapidAPI: fresh-linkedin-profile-data.p.rapidapi.com
  • Local: Ashby dedupe, candidate cache dedup
  • Bay Area location set (58 cities)

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
Environment variable manager for FORGE.

File: core/config.py | Lines: ~150+

docs.title: What It Does
docs.text: Loads and manages all environment variables from .env file. Centralizes API keys, Discord IDs, model selections, and service endpoints. No secrets hardcoded in source; all externalized. Loads once at startup and provides module-level constants.

docs.title: Key Configuration Variables
docs.text: Category Variables Discord DISCORD_BOT_TOKEN, DISCORD_GUILD_ID, DISCORD_CHANNEL_ID, MONITOR_CHANNEL_ID, ZACK_ID Claude API ANTHROPIC_API_KEY, DEFAULT_MODEL Models MODELS (dict: haiku, sonnet, opus) Search BRAVE_API_KEY, TAVILY_API_KEY, EXA_API_KEY, SERPER_API_KEY Browser COMET_PATH Google APIs GOOGLE_CLIENT_SECRETS, GOOGLE_TOKEN_PATH Third-party NOTTE_API_KEY, GEMINI_API_KEY, OPENAI_API_KEY, APOLLO_API_KEY, SUPADATA_API_KEY Logging LOG_LEVEL, LOG_DIR

docs.title: Key Functions
docs.text: Function Purpose load_dotenv() Load .env file on import now_pst() Current time in Pacific Time (used throughout FORGE)

docs.title: Secret Policy
docs.text:
• NEVER hardcode secrets in source
  • ALWAYS use environment variables
  • APIFY_API_TOKEN removed (2026) — use RapidAPI instead

docs.title: Copilot Module
docs.text:
Claude Code persistent pipe (Holy Grail Edition) — bidirectional stdin/stdout with concurrent message queue and auto-recovery.

File: modules/copilot.py | Lines: ~1,701

docs.title: What It Does
docs.text: Spawns a persistent Claude Code CLI process and maintains a bidirectional JSON-line pipe for instant code-level automation. Handles message queueing, auto-recovery on crash, health monitoring, and context building. Near-instant responses after first boot (10s initial).

docs.title: Key Functions
docs.text: Function Purpose start_copilot() Spawn Claude Code process, initialize pipe send_to_copilot() Queue user message, stream response to Discord get_copilot_response() Execute tool call, return structured result health_monitor() Periodic check: restart if dead (max 5 retries) stop_copilot() Graceful shutdown _build_context() Assemble FORGE state, session history for Claude

docs.title: Dependencies
docs.text:
• Claude Code CLI: claude.cmd (Max subscription, OAuth)
  • subprocess.Popen — process management, stdin/stdout pipes
  • Config: data/copilot_session.json (session state)
  • PID file: data/copilot_pid.json
  • MCP config: data/copilot_mcp.json
  • Context builder: core.copilot_context

docs.title: Related
docs.text:
• Skill: /copilot — user-facing command
  • Brain integration: Brain Module
  • Task queue: data/cc_queue.json (delegated work)

docs.title: Database
docs.text:
Shared SQLite connection pool with health checks.

File: core/database.py | Lines: ~150+

docs.title: What It Does
docs.text: Manages the shared aiosqlite connection to data/forge.db. All FORGE features share this single database. Provides connection pooling with health checks (detects stale connections and reconnects), automatic table initialization, and WAL mode for concurrent writes.

docs.title: Key Functions
docs.text: Function Purpose get_db() Get or create shared connection, health check close_db() Close connection and cleanup init_tables() Create all tables for every feature (idempotent)

docs.title: Database Tables
docs.text: Table Purpose conversation_messages Store conversation history (channel_id, role, content) (defined in init_tables) Many feature-specific tables auto-created on startup

docs.title: Connection Features
docs.text:
• Health check — verify connection is alive before use
  • WAL mode — concurrent writers enabled
  • Busy timeout — 5000ms wait for locks
  • Row factory — return rows as dict-like objects

docs.text:

docs.title: Event Bus
docs.text:
Cross-tool intelligence with causation tracking and correlation IDs.

File: core/event_bus.py | Lines: ~250+

docs.title: What It Does
docs.text: Lightweight async event bus connecting FORGE's independent systems (Agent Lab, Pipeline, GitHub Scout, Knowledge Base). Each event carries causation chain (parent_event_id), correlation ID (groups related events), and source attribution. Persists to SQLite for crash recovery and analytics. Bounded async queue prevents backlog.

docs.title: Key Components
docs.text: Component Purpose Event Typed dataclass with event_type, data, source_module, causation IDs, timestamp emit() Broadcast event, return event_id, optionally set parent_event_id on() Register handler for event type (decorator) get_chain() Query causation chain from root to event _persist() Save event to SQLite for recovery

docs.title: Event Example
docs.text:
event_id = await event_bus.emit(
    "candidate_found",
    {"name": "Jane", "source": "github", "score": 4.2},
    source_module="pipeline_scout",
    parent_event_id=strategy_event_id,
)

docs.title: Key Features
docs.text:
• Causation chains — track root cause through nested events
  • Correlation IDs — group related event chains
  • Subscriber isolation — one failed handler doesn't crash others
  • Persistence — SQLite backup for crash recovery + analytics

docs.title: Google Services Integration
docs.text:
Gmail and Google Calendar via OAuth2 with multi-account support.

File: modules/google_services.py | Lines: ~1551

docs.title: What It Does
docs.text: Manages Gmail and Google Calendar access via OAuth2. Supports default account plus labeled additional accounts (e.g., hiring@company, candidate@domain). Handles credential refresh, sends emails with attachments, creates calendar events, and syncs calendar data. Multi-account registry stored in JSON tracks token paths per label.

docs.title: Key Functions
docs.text: Function Purpose get_credentials(account="default") Load or refresh OAuth2 token send_email(to, subject, body, attachments, account) Send email via Gmail API create_calendar_event(title, start, end, attendees, account) Create calendar event list_calendar_events(start, end, account) Fetch events in range get_gmail_threads(query, limit, account) Search threads with query _load_account_registry() Load account label → token path mapping

docs.title: Dependencies
docs.text:
• google-auth-oauthlib — OAuth2 flow
  • google-auth-httplib2 — API client
  • google-api-python-client — Gmail + Calendar APIs
  • pickle — token serialization
  • Setup: client_secrets.json from Google Cloud Console

docs.title: Knowledge Base (RAG)
docs.text:
Semantic search and document embedding with ChromaDB for local QA over uploaded documents.

File: modules/knowledge_base.py | Lines: ~880

docs.title: What It Does
docs.text: Implements RAG (Retrieval Augmented Generation) for FORGE. Ingest PDF, DOCX, TXT, MD files into the knowledge/ directory. Documents are chunked (500 tokens), embedded locally with ONNX (no GPU), and indexed in ChromaDB. Semantic search returns relevant chunks to augment Claude's responses. Watchdog monitors directory for new files and auto-ingests them.

docs.title: Key Functions
docs.text: Function Purpose init_knowledge_base(bot, channel_id) Lazy-load ChromaDB, create DB table, start file watcher search_knowledge(query, limit) Semantic search — returns top N relevant chunks ingest_document(file_path) Parse, chunk, embed, and store document get_kb_context(query) Format search results for system prompt injection

docs.title: Dependencies
docs.text:
• ChromaDB — local vector DB with ONNX embeddings (no GPU needed)
  • watchdog — file monitoring for auto-ingest
  • Database: kb_documents table tracks ingested files
  • Cache: LRU (50 entries, 5min TTL) to avoid redundant searches

docs.title: Related
docs.text:
• Brain System — injects KB context into system prompt
  • Document Processing

docs.title: LinkedIn Operations & PRISM Scoring
docs.text:
FORGE LinkedIn profile lookup, batch scraping, PRISM evaluation, candidate database operations, Stack Overflow scraping, and the List Runner.

File: modules/linkedin_ops.py | Lines: ~2,737

docs.title: What It Does
docs.text: Orchestrates all LinkedIn profile sourcing, PRISM candidate evaluation, and database persistence. Handles batch scraping via Apify, manual/paste profile evaluation with Claude Sonnet scoring, Stack Overflow scraper integration, and the List Runner for continuous company scanning.

docs.title: Key Functions
docs.text: Function Purpose prism_evaluate() Score candidate profile against active JD using Claude Sonnet batch_prism() Batch-evaluate multiple profiles from queue linkedin_search() Search LinkedIn profiles and cache URLs add_to_candidate_db() Persist scored candidate to SQLite get_candidate_by_profile() Fetch candidate from DB by profile URL list_runner() Scan target company LinkedIn pages, find employees, queue URLs stack_overflow_lookup() Fetch SO profile from URL

docs.title: News Aggregator
docs.text:
RSS feed aggregation with Discord integration — polls news sources and posts new articles.

File: modules/news.py | Lines: ~610

docs.title: What It Does
docs.text: Polls 20+ RSS sources (WaPo, Reuters, AP, BBC, etc.) every 30 minutes. Deduplicates via SQLite (tracks seen articles, 7-day retention). Posts new articles to 📰-news Discord channel as rich embeds with source branding, category emoji, and clickable links. Supports filtering by category (world, tech, national security, etc.).

docs.title: Key Functions
docs.text: Function Purpose init_news_aggregator(bot, channel_id) Start polling loop, create DB table poll_sources() Fetch all RSS feeds, parse, dedupe post_article(article, source) Format embed and post to Discord _clean_seen_cache() Remove articles older than 7 days add_rss_source(name, url, category) Register custom RSS feed

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:

  • Max Posts Per Poll: 5
  • Sources: 20+ default (WaPo, AP, Reuters, CNN, BBC, etc.)
  • Categories: world, national, politics, business, tech, investigative, national security
  • Embed Colors: Per-source branding (WaPo: #2B2B2B, AP: #E3120B, etc.)

docs.title: Outreach Drafter
docs.text:
Generates personalized LinkedIn/email messages for candidates on behalf of HockeyStack.

docs.title: What It Does
docs.text: Drafts 2 variant outreach messages (Direct and Rapport-first) for candidates. Operates as a thin wrapper around brain (Claude). Enforces HockeyStack voice: conversational, specific to candidate's background, no corporate jargon. Includes soft CTAs without time commitments or overused phrases.

docs.title: Key Functions
docs.text: Function Purpose draft_outreach(brain, candidate_profile, role, channel, tone) Generate message variants

  • Input: candidate profile, role, channel (email/InMail), tone (casual/formal)
  • Output: 2 message variants with subject lines (if applicable)
  • Model params: max_tokens=1500, temperature=0.6

docs.title: Overnight Engine
docs.text:
Make VPS's 10 idle hours productive.

File: core/overnight_engine.py | Lines: ~300+

docs.title: What It Does
docs.text: Instead of sleeping, overnight engine plans and executes deep research tasks during VPS downtime (9:30 PM, 12:30 AM, 3:30 AM, 6:30 AM Pacific). Task types: continue yesterday's sourcing (enrich pipelines, score unscoreds), deep candidate research (GitHub activity, blog posts), company intelligence, stale outreach follow-ups, knowledge base maintenance. Each cycle picks highest-priority task, executes up to 30 min, cost-capped at $2/night.

docs.title: Key Functions
docs.text: Function Purpose register(scheduler, bot) Register overnight cycles with APScheduler (4 times/night) run_cycle() Pick highest-priority task, execute, log results get_last_night_summary() Return what was accomplished _pick_task() Select next task based on priority and progress _estimate_cost() Check if task would exceed nightly cap

docs.title: Task Priority Order
docs.text:
1. Continue yesterday's sourcing — enrich partial pipelines, score unscoreds
  2. Deep candidate research — GitHub activity, blog posts, open source
  3. Company intelligence — build profiles for target companies
  4. Stale outreach follow-ups — identify who needs a nudge
  5. Knowledge base maintenance — re-index docs, prune duplicates

docs.title: Cost Tracking
docs.text:
• Nightly cap: $2.00 USD (generous, actual spend much lower)
  • Company raids: ~$0.003/candidate (PRISM + Serper + RapidAPI)
  • State file: data/overnight_state.json

docs.title: Pipeline Agent 3: Enricher
docs.text:
Drains url_queue, Haiku pre-filter, Apify batch-scrape, post-filter, push to profile_queue.

File: modules/pipeline_enricher.py | Lines: ~379

docs.title: What It Does
docs.text: Stage 3 of the sourcing pipeline. Receives LinkedIn URLs from Scout, batches them for efficiency, pre-filters with Claude Haiku (quick signal check), scrapes full profiles via Apify, applies post-filter rules, and queues enriched profiles for the Evaluator agent.

docs.title: Key Functions
docs.text: Function Purpose enricher_agent() Main loop: batch → pre-filter → scrape → post-filter → queue _haiku_prefilter() Quick candidate signal check (experience, tech stack) _dedup_batch() Remove duplicate URLs in batch _cost_gate() Prompt Zack for cost confirmation if batch exceeds threshold _flush() Process accumulated batch (pre-filter, scrape, post-filter)

docs.title: Dependencies
docs.text:
• asyncio.Queue — inter-agent communication
  • core.sourcing_filters.post_filter_profiles — exclusion rules
  • Apify API (APIFY_API_TOKEN), actor: supreme_coder~linkedin-profile-scraper
  • Claude Haiku (MODELS) — pre-filtering signal check
  • Cost tracking: $0.003/profile default

docs.title: Related
docs.text:
• Producer: Pipeline Scout
  • Consumer: Pipeline Evaluator
  • Parent: Sourcing Pipeline

docs.title: Pipeline Agent 4: Evaluator
docs.text:
Drains full profiles from profile_queue, PRISM-scores with Sonnet, generates timing signals + outreach context, saves to DB, posts results to Discord.

File: modules/pipeline_evaluator.py | Lines: ~435

docs.title: What It Does
docs.text: Final pipeline stage. Receives enriched full profiles, scores each with PRISM (Claude Sonnet), extracts outreach timing signals and context, persists to candidate database, and streams top results to Discord. Runs until sentinel received.

docs.title: Key Functions
docs.text: Function Purpose evaluator_agent() Main loop: buffer profiles → batch score → post results _score_batch() PRISM-score 10 profiles with Sonnet in parallel _extract_timing_signals() Parse score report for contact window, optimal approach _save_to_db() Store scored candidate (profile, score, notes, timing) _post_results() Send top candidates to Discord as rich embeds

docs.title: Dependencies
docs.text:

docs.title: Pipeline Graph
docs.text:
Lightweight workflow engine replacing LangGraph.

File: core/pipeline_graph.py | Lines: ~400+

docs.title: What It Does
docs.text: StateGraph-like API without LangChain/LangGraph dependencies. Typed state (TypedDict with Annotated list reducers), node registration with retry policies, conditional edges (routing functions), SQLite checkpointing for crash recovery, and async execution via ainvoke(). All new FORGE workflows use this.

docs.title: Key Components
docs.text: Component Purpose StateGraph Define state type, add nodes and edges, compile, execute Node Function wrapper with retry policy (max_attempts, backoff) RetryPolicy Configure max_attempts, initial_interval, backoff_factor ainvoke() Execute graph async, return final state get_state() Retrieve saved state from checkpoint

docs.title: Workflow Example
docs.text:
graph = StateGraph(MyState)
graph.add_node("step1", step1_func, retry_policy=RetryPolicy(max_attempts=3))
graph.add_edge("step1", "step2")
graph.add_conditional_edges("step2", router_func, {"next": "step3", "end": END})
compiled = graph.compile(checkpoint_db="data/checkpoints.db")
result = await compiled.ainvoke(initial_state)

docs.title: Key Features
docs.text:
• Typed state — TypedDict with reducer functions
  • Conditional routing — next step depends on current state
  • Retry policies — exponential backoff on failure
  • Checkpointing — SQLite persistence, resume from checkpoint
  • Zero LangChain — pure Python, works on 3.14

docs.title: Related
docs.text:
• Event Bus
  • Autonomy Loop
  • Overnight Engine

docs.title: PRISM Candidate Evaluator
docs.text:
Comprehensive candidate scoring engine using leadership signals, tech stack matching, and FastEval framework.

File: modules/prism.py | Lines: ~728

docs.title: What It Does
docs.text: PRISM scores candidates 1-5 based on tech fit, experience, and leadership signals. Detects and quantifies management experience (team size, hiring, mentoring), extracts tech stack compatibility with JD requirements, and produces structured YES/NO/MAYBE verdicts. The module implements the glassattempt Chrome extension scoring logic for talent evaluation.

docs.title: Key Functions
docs.text: Function Purpose evaluate_candidate(profile, jd, role) Main scoring function — returns 1-5 score with signals _detect_leadership_signals(profile) Extracts and scores management experience (teams led, size, hiring) _detect_leadership_role(jd) Checks if JD requires leadership capability _extract_tech_stack(jd) Dynamically parses required tech from JD text _score_tech_fit(profile, required_techs) Calculates tech compatibility (0-100)

docs.title: Dependencies
docs.text:
• logging, json, re — standard utilities
  • Internal: uses FastEval prompt format
  • No external APIs — purely local evaluation

docs.title: Related
docs.text:
• Sourcing Pipeline
  • Outreach Module
  • Memory: rules_prism_scoring_mandatory.md

docs.title: Scheduler Module
docs.text:
Scheduled tasks, reminders, and recurring jobs using APScheduler.

File: modules/scheduler.py | Lines: ~750

docs.title: What It Does
docs.text: Manages all FORGE scheduled tasks: reminders, recurring briefings, morning reports, wiki recompile, cookie cleanup. Loads tasks from DB at startup, registers cron jobs with APScheduler, catches up on missed jobs (e.g., if bot restarted during scheduled window), cleans up expired one-time reminders.

docs.title: Key Functions
docs.text: Function Purpose init_scheduler() Start APScheduler, load DB tasks, register built-in cron jobs add_reminder() Create one-time reminder at specific time add_recurring_task() Create cron-based recurring task (e.g., daily 7am) list_tasks() Get all active scheduled jobs cancel_task() Remove a scheduled task _catch_up_missed_jobs() Execute any jobs that were missed during downtime _cleanup_past_reminders() Disable expired one-time reminders

docs.title: Dependencies
docs.text:
• apscheduler — cron job management, async support
  • core.database.get_db — persist tasks to DB
  • Timezone: Pacific Time (America/Los_Angeles)
  • Built-in jobs: wiki recompile (6am), cookie cleanup (hourly)
  • Grace time: 3600s (misfires coalesced, don't duplicate)

docs.title: Related
docs.text:
• Task DB: scheduled_tasks table
  • Brain integration: Morning briefing via Brain Module
  • Config: Scheduler Config

docs.title: Self-Healing
docs.text:
Autonomous recovery, escalation, and strategy adaptation.

File: core/self_healing.py | Lines: ~300+

docs.title: What It Does
docs.text: Bridges detection and action: FORGE observes problems and automatically fixes them without waiting for manual intervention. Four components: cog auto-recovery (reloads crashed cogs), friction escalation (promotes friction patterns to rules), failure adaptor (adjusts circuit breaker thresholds), and health pulse (periodic self-check). Runs on 30-minute intervals + event-driven via Super Grail.

docs.title: Key Functions
docs.text: Function Purpose init(bot, scheduler, monitor_channel_id) Initialize self-healer with bot and scheduler run_friction_escalation() Promote friction patterns to enforced rules (6-hourly) run_failure_adaptation() Adjust circuit breaker thresholds (30-minute) run_health_pulse() Periodic self-check, report to monitor detect_crashed_cogs() Find cogs that failed to load or crashed recover_cog(cog_name) Dynamically reload a cog

docs.title: Recovery Loop
docs.text:
1. Monitor detects crash or friction pattern
  2. Self-Healer reads detection, identifies root cause
  3. Recovery executes fix (reload cog, adjust thresholds, promote rule)
  4. Verify checks if fix worked, reports to monitor

docs.title: Sourcing Pipeline Orchestrator
docs.text:
4-Agent orchestrator: creates queues, wires agents (Scout, Enricher, Analyzer, Evaluator), manages checkpoints and live dashboard.

File: modules/sourcing_pipeline.py | Lines: ~782

docs.title: What It Does
docs.text: Multi-stage async pipeline for candidate discovery and evaluation. Accepts search criteria (GitHub repos, Slack communities, companies), spins up 4 independent agents with inter-queue handoff, monitors progress in real-time dashboard, persists checkpoint state, and gracefully shuts down or force-cancels on request.

docs.title: Key Functions
docs.text: Function Purpose run_pipeline() Start full 4-agent pipeline with given config dry_run_pipeline() Strategist-only preview, returns plan for approval get_pipeline_status() Live snapshot of all agent stats stop_pipeline() Graceful shutdown of running agents kill_pipeline() Force-cancel pipeline session get_pipeline_config() Load config from DB (or defaults) set_pipeline_config() Persist config updates

  • core.checkpoint.CheckpointManager — session state persistence
  • core.config.now_pst, safe_create_task

  • DB: pipeline_config table for persistent settings

docs.title: Multi-Sport Props Analyzer
docs.text:
NBA/NHL/MLB player prop evaluation against PrizePicks lines using real-time stats.

File: modules/sports_props.py | Lines: ~1664

docs.title: What It Does
docs.text: Fetches live player props from PrizePicks partner API, pulls real-time stats from NBA Stats, NHL API, and MLB Baseball Reference, and identifies edges where model projection differs from line. Supports 3-leg and 4-leg entries. Computes L10 averages, medians, standard deviation, and hit rates. Cost: FREE (partner API + free stats).

docs.title: Key Functions
docs.text: Function Purpose fetch_props(league, stat_type) Get current player props from PrizePicks get_player_stats(player, team, league, days) Fetch recent stats for player compute_projection(values) Calculate weighted average (recency-weighted) analyze_edge(prop, recent_stats) Compare projection vs line, flag edges get_board() Full prop board with all current plays

docs.title: Dependencies
docs.text:
• httpx — sync/async HTTP for API calls
  • PrizePicks: partner-api.prizepicks.com (free, no auth)
  • NBA Stats: stats.nba.com with headers
  • NHL: api-web.nhle.com/v1
  • MLB: baseballrefence.com via scrape

docs.text:

docs.title: Sports Swarm Evaluator
docs.text:
4-agent consensus evaluation for player props — stats, matchup, trends, and contrarian analysts.

File: modules/sports_swarm.py | Lines: ~344

docs.title: What It Does
docs.text: Multi-agent consensus system for prop picks. Four independent Haiku agents (Stats Analyst, Matchup Analyst, Trends Analyst, Contrarian Analyst) evaluate a prop independently. A judge synthesizes all opinions into final verdict (OVER/UNDER/SKIP). Cost per pick: ~$0.02 (4 Haiku + 1 judge call). Uses lab API key for cheap inference.

docs.title: Key Functions
docs.text: Function Purpose evaluate_pick(prop_data) Main entry — run all 4 agents + judge _get_client() Get Anthropic async client (lab or regular API key) _run_stats_agent(prop_data) Pure numbers analysis (L10 avg, median, variance) _run_matchup_agent(prop_data) Opponent defense and pace analysis _run_trends_agent(prop_data) Hot/cold streaks, rest, home/away splits _run_contrarian_agent(prop_data) Public bias, line movement, trap lines _synthesize_verdict(all_opinions) Judge finalizes consensus

docs.title: Dependencies
docs.text:
• anthropic — async client
  • ANTHROPIC_LAB_API_KEY or ANTHROPIC_API_KEY
  • Model: claude-haiku-4-5-20251001 (ultra-cheap, fast)

docs.title: Text-to-Speech (TTS)
docs.text:
ElevenLabs voice synthesis with daily voice rotation and history tracking.

File: modules/tts.py | Lines: ~275

docs.title: What It Does
docs.text: Converts text to speech using ElevenLabs API. Rotates through available voices daily — never repeats the same voice twice in a row. Tracks voice history in JSON, supports both fast (turbo_v2_5) and high-quality (multilingual_v2) models. Used for morning briefs, daily picks summaries, and Discord audio messages.

docs.title: Key Functions
docs.text: Function Purpose get_available_voices() Fetch all ElevenLabs voices, cache as needed pick_daily_voice(voices) Select next voice — avoids last voice, cycles pool text_to_speech(text, voice_id) Synthesize audio file via ElevenLabs API _load_voice_history() Load voice rotation state from disk _save_voice_history(history) Persist voice usage history

docs.title: Dependencies
docs.text:
• aiohttp — async HTTP for ElevenLabs API
  • ELEVENLABS_API_KEY from .env
  • Models: eleven_turbo_v2_5 (fast, default) or eleven_multilingual_v2 (quality)

docs.title: Meeting Analysis: HockeyStack Team Sync w/ Arda & Hank
🔑 TL;DR
docs.text: The team is refining their sourcing strategy for a ClickHouse database engineer role by widening the search net to include anyone with ClickHouse keywords (removing strict title filters), excluding only managers and site reliability engineers, while avoiding Altinity but including ClickHouse company candidates.

docs.title: 📋 Action Items
docs.text:
Zack - Remove strict job title filters and search primarily by ClickHouse keyword on LinkedIn Recruiter - Add software engineer to title searches when using title filters - Exclude manager and site reliability engineer titles from searches - Remove Altinity candidates from sourcing list - Include ClickHouse company employees in candidate pool - Source candidates from Netherlands/Amsterdam area from GitHub scrape - Provide list of Altinity-like companies to Arda and Hank - Re-run searches on Series A-C companies using broader ClickHouse keyword approach - Implement the LinkedIn scraping API that Arda provided - Ask questions in the DM group if stuck on API implementation

Arda - Provide feedback on the job description (expected next week)

Hank - Review and finalize job description for release next week

Unassigned - None

docs.title: ✅ Decisions Made
docs.text:
• Widen the sourcing net by removing strict title constraints and focusing on ClickHouse as a keyword
  • Exclude managers and site reliability engineers from searches
  • Accept candidates with 1+ years of ClickHouse experience if they worked at scale
  • Accept candidates who use multiple databases alongside ClickHouse (e.g., Kubernetes + MySQL/Postgres)
  • Do not source from Altinity, but DO source from ClickHouse company itself
  • Include candidates from Netherlands/Amsterdam in sourcing efforts
  • Use exclusion-based filtering (removing unwanted titles) rather than inclusion-based filtering
  • Rely on job description and self-selection to help filter candidates once outreach begins
  • Leverage automation (LinkedIn API) rather than manual work where possible

docs.title: 💬 Talking Points
docs.text:
• Zack has sourced 35-40 candidates so far, finding it challenging due to the rarity of 5+ years ClickHouse experience and unclear job titles
  • Most GitHub repository contributors are Russia-based, limiting the viable candidate pool from that source
  • LinkedIn Recruiter profiles show more detail than standard profiles, making it difficult for Arda/Hank to see what Zack sees during calibration
  • The team discussed whether candidates need ClickHouse exclusively vs. experience with adjacent technologies (Kafka, Postgres, MySQL, OLAP/OLTP databases)
  • Max (team member) was used as an example of someone who has ClickHouse skills but doesn't elaborate on it in their LinkedIn profile, highlighting the need for broader keyword searches

docs.title: 📌 Weekly Action List
docs.text:
• Modify LinkedIn Recruiter search strategy to use ClickHouse keyword without title constraints (Owner: Zack, Priority: High)
  • Add exclusion filters for managers and site reliability engineers (Owner: Zack, Priority: High)
  • Remove Altinity candidates from current sourcing list (Owner: Zack, Priority: High)
  • Re-run searches on Series A-C companies with new broader approach (Owner: Zack, Priority: High)
  • Source Netherlands/Amsterdam candidates from GitHub scrape (Owner: Zack, Priority: Medium)
  • Implement LinkedIn scraping API for automation (Owner: Zack, Priority: Medium)
  • Compile and share list of Altinity-like companies (Owner: Zack, Priority: Medium)
  • Finalize and release job description (Owner: Hank/Arda, Priority: High)

docs.title: ⏰ Deadlines Mentioned
docs.text: • Next week — Job description expected to be finalized and released (Owner: Arda/Hank)

docs.title: ❓ Unresolved Questions
docs.text:
• Exact percentage of Russia-based candidates from GitHub scrape (rough estimate given, but not precise)
  • Whether to implement pre-screen emails before scheduling calls (discussed but not decided)
  • Full list of companies similar to Altinity that should be avoided (Zack to compile and share)

docs.title: 📝 Quick Summary
docs.text: Zack provided an update on sourcing efforts for a ClickHouse database engineer role, having sourced 35-40 candidates primarily from GitHub scrapes and companies listed on ClickHouse's website. The team decided to significantly broaden the search strategy by removing strict job title filters and focusing on ClickHouse as a keyword, while excluding only managers and site reliability engineers. They clarified that candidates with 1+ years of ClickHouse experience at scale are acceptable, as are those with adjacent database technologies, and that sourcing from ClickHouse company itself is encouraged while Altinity should be avoided.

docs.title: Meeting — February 27, 2026 at 09:42 AM
docs.text: Field Value Date February 27, 2026 at 09:42 AM Duration ~31 minutes Speakers Arda, Hank

docs.title: Meeting Analysis: HockeyStack Team Sync w/ Arda & Hank
🔑 TL;DR
docs.text: The team is preparing to launch outreach campaigns for two engineering roles (ClickHouse Staff Engineer and Backend AI/LLM Engineer), with Zack having sourced 140 strong candidates for ClickHouse and planning to split time 60/40 between the two roles while A/B testing salary disclosure in messaging.

docs.title: 📋 Action Items
docs.text:
Zack - Revise ClickHouse outreach sequence based on Arda's feedback (add bullet points, reframe title as "Staff Engineer," incorporate AI/scaling challenges) - Create A/B test: 50% of candidates receive salary info, 50% don't - Change salary range to "up to 300K base" (instead of 240-400K range) - Send DQ message to Umberto - Tag Leo to review Leroy's take-home assignment - Send take-home to Liz Wright at 11am - Message Jessica about creating week-over-week response rate reporting by sequence - Start sourcing for Backend AI/LLM Engineer role (40% time allocation) once job description is ready - Launch ClickHouse outreach campaign once Arda approves final messaging

Arda - Leave detailed comments on ClickHouse messaging document - Write requirements/brain dump for Backend AI/LLM-focused engineer role - Reach out to Jason to reschedule meeting (was scheduled for 4:30pm their time/6pm)

Hank - Work with Zack to update Backend AI/LLM job description once Arda provides requirements - Check with Jessica if week-over-week response rate report already exists - Verify with HR whether salary disclosure is required in outreach messages

Unassigned - Set up Google Calendar room booking system for the office

docs.title: ✅ Decisions Made
docs.text:
• DQ Umberto from Product Support Engineer role (referral from Eli; mixed technical feedback from Roy and Nevan, no standout qualities)
  • Split Zack's time 60% ClickHouse sourcing, 40% Backend AI/LLM Engineer sourcing
  • A/B test salary disclosure in ClickHouse outreach: 50% with salary, 50% without
  • Change salary positioning to "up to 300K base" for maximum flexibility
  • Remove San Francisco office mention from messaging (keep fully remote)
  • Reframe ClickHouse role title as "Staff Engineer" with ClickHouse focus rather than limiting title
  • Prioritize getting response rates higher for ClickHouse role due to limited candidate pool

docs.title: 💬 Talking Points
docs.text:
• ClickHouse sourcing progress: Zack doubled candidate pipeline from ~70 to 140 strong US-based candidates after calibration sessions, using improved JuiceBox criteria filtering and company list scraping
  • Outreach messaging optimization: Need to improve response rates by adding bullet points, incorporating AI/scaling challenges, and testing salary disclosure approaches
  • Product Support Engineer progress: 12 interviews in 2 weeks with strong candidate quality; most progressing to take-home stage; Jason performed well
  • New Backend AI/LLM role: Opening second search to split Zack's time, targeting recently laid-off talent from tech companies
  • Office space challenges: Sales team occupying conference rooms; need for Google Calendar room booking system

docs.title: 📌 Weekly Action List
docs.text:

  • Reschedule Jason meeting (Owner: Arda, Priority: Medium)

docs.title: ⏰ Deadlines Mentioned

  • 4:30 PM today (their time) — Jason's originally scheduled meeting with Arda (needs rescheduling)
  • Today — Message Jessica about response rate reporting (Zack)

docs.title: ❓ Unresolved Questions

  • Are there legal requirements for salary disclosure in outreach messages (vs. job postings)?
  • What specific qualifications and requirements does Arda want for the Backend AI/LLM-focused engineer role?
  • Should the team target specific recently laid-off engineers from known companies?

docs.title: 📝 Quick Summary
docs.text: Zack successfully doubled his ClickHouse candidate pipeline to 140 strong US-based profiles using improved sourcing techniques and JuiceBox criteria filtering. The team is now preparing to launch outreach campaigns with revised messaging that emphasizes AI/scaling challenges and A/B tests salary disclosure to improve response rates. Additionally, they're opening a second search for a Backend AI/LLM Engineer role, with Zack splitting time 60/40 between the two positions, while continuing to progress Product Support Engineer candidates through the interview pipeline.

docs.title: 🔑 TL;DR
docs.text: The ClickHouse engineer recruitment campaign achieved exceptional response rates (9 replies, 5 calls scheduled from first 100 emails), prompting a strategic pause on further outreach while the team shifts focus to preparing for sourcing LLM/agentic AI engineers.

docs.title: 📋 Action Items
docs.text:
Zack - Hold the second batch (100 emails) of ClickHouse engineer outreach - Post the ClickHouse engineer JD on Ashby job board - Archive candidates: Riley Cashin, William K - Respond to H-1B candidate inquiry (confirm sponsorship for qualified candidates) - Begin prep/research phase for LLM/agentic AI engineer sourcing - Research Sierra and other agentic AI companies to understand the role requirements - Collaborate with Arda in channel to define filters and search approach for LLM engineer role - Follow up with Tommy Kendo (take-home due Saturday)

Hank - Talk to Jason today about his status/next steps - Speak with Byron this week - Follow up with McKay Archibald today - Get Claude Code organization account set up for Zack - Await Arda's feedback on JSON decision before resuming product support engineer sourcing

Unassigned - Wait for Arda's decision on JSON (impacts product support engineer hiring strategy)

docs.title: ✅ Decisions Made
docs.text:
• Pause sending second batch of ClickHouse engineer emails due to strong response rate from first batch
  • Adopt a more controlled, thoughtful recruiting approach rather than "old-school playbooks" of mass outreach
  • Pause product support engineer sourcing until Arda provides feedback on JSON
  • Will sponsor H-1B visas for highly qualified candidates
  • Archive/close candidates: Riley Cashin (already a manager, won't take role), William K (no response after follow-up)
  • Next recruiting focus will be LLM/agentic AI engineer (prep phase starting now)

docs.title: 💬 Talking Points
docs.text:
• ClickHouse engineer campaign success: 9 replies and 5 scheduled calls from only 100 emails sent, with more responses still expected
  • Philosophy shift in recruiting: moving away from traditional high-volume outreach to more strategic, efficient, and thoughtful sourcing enabled by custom tooling
  • Product support engineer pipeline challenges: Liz withdrew (received manager promotion), Jason likely disqualified based on Arda's feedback
  • Candidate pipeline review: closed loop on previous week's candidates, archived non-responsive or unsuitable candidates
  • Next phase preparation: researching and developing sourcing strategy for LLM/agentic AI engineer role, starting with companies like Sierra

docs.title: 📝 Quick Summary
docs.text: Zack and Hank reviewed recruiting progress, celebrating exceptional response rates for the ClickHouse engineer role (9 replies, 5 calls from 100 emails). They decided to pause further outreach and adopt a more strategic, efficient recruiting approach rather than traditional high-volume methods. The team closed the loop on previous candidates, archiving non-responsive or unsuitable prospects, and is now entering a prep phase to develop sourcing strategies for an LLM/agentic AI engineer role while awaiting decisions on current product support engineer candidates.

docs.title: ✅ Decisions Made

  • The draft JD will be posted in the tactical channel rather than sent directly to Zack

docs.text: Zack and Hank worked on revising the job description for a new Backend Engineer role focused on agentic AI and LLM development. They discovered that previous updates to job postings hadn't saved properly and decided to create a fresh Ashby project for this role. Zack provided key direction on the mission statement (building a single portal for GTM teams using AI agents) and outlined technical responsibilities including agent orchestration, memory context handling, and multi-model optimization. Hank will use Claude/ChatGPT to complete the draft and post it to the tactical channel for review.

docs.title: 🔑 TL;DR
docs.text: Hank and Zack are moving forward with a new "agents backend engineer" job description and outreach campaign, with Matt from Twilio scheduled to interview with Arda on Thursday, while establishing a new structured Friday meeting format focused on metrics and pipeline analysis.

docs.title: 📋 Action Items
docs.text:
Zack - Review and finalize the agents backend engineer JD and sequences posted in the tactical channel - Start outreach campaign tomorrow (pending Arda's approval tonight) - Schedule candidate interviews for next week, capping at 5 interviews maximum for Hank per week - Spread interviews throughout the week when possible (one per day ideally) - Develop a repeatable Friday meeting agenda covering: metrics, tooling updates, and strategic initiatives - Prepare week-over-week pipeline metrics with percentage changes for each funnel stage - Provide color and context when presenting metrics (explain why numbers changed, what worked/didn't work) - Review Jessica's Ashby reports and graphs for funnel stage analysis - Continue using Prism evaluator tool for candidate filtering

Hank - Conduct interview with Matt on Thursday at 5:30 PM - Review the agents backend engineer JD and sequences tonight - Conduct up to 5 interviews next week (spread throughout the week) - Determine which other candidates should talk to Arda next week - Complete compliance-related work (ongoing)

Arda - Review and approve the agents backend engineer JD tonight - Interview Matt on Thursday at 5:30 PM - Determine salary range for the agents backend engineer role

Unassigned - Set up Calendly configuration as Arda requested - Decide whether to include compensation details in outreach

docs.title: ✅ Decisions Made
docs.text:
• Using "agents backend engineer" as the job title (instead of AI or LLM engineer) for the new role
  • Capping Hank's interviews at 5 per week maximum to manage workload
  • Not scheduling Hank interviews this week due to workload
  • Launching the agents backend engineer campaign tomorrow (pending Arda's approval)
  • Candidates responding mid-week will be scheduled for the following week
  • Implementing a structured, repeatable Friday meeting format with Arda focused on metrics first
  • Continuing the non-traditional recruiting model where Hank (VP of Engineering) conducts first technical screens
  • Continuing to target Twilio employees as a strategic sourcing thread

docs.title: 💬 Talking Points
docs.text:
• Matt (from Twilio) is a strong candidate who booked the earliest available time with Arda and is still interviewing despite having multiple offers from big-name companies
  • The recruiting model of having Hank conduct initial technical screens is proving effective for Series A/B stage, allowing authentic conversations with younger engineers who appreciate transparency over traditional recruiting processes
  • Zack has developed a tool called "Prism" that serves as a second brain for candidate evaluation, helping filter through ~1,000 profiles daily
  • The company's new focus on "agents" provides a compelling story for recruiting, which is crucial for attracting talent beyond just compensation
  • Friday meetings with Arda need to be restructured with a repeatable agenda starting with metrics, then tooling updates, followed by strategic context and color commentary

docs.title: Meeting Analysis: Eng Recruiting Sync w/ Arda
🔑 TL;DR
docs.text: The AI engineer role is experiencing poor response rates (1 out of 192 sequences), prompting a strategic shift to personalized outreach using AI-powered customization based on candidates' GitHub/LinkedIn profiles, while continuing ClickHouse recruiting with A/B testing on compensation messaging.

docs.title: 📋 Action Items
docs.text:
Zack - Create a Notion-based agenda document for recurring meetings (reuse Casey's doc format) - Build AI-powered personalization system using Claude Opus 4.6 with decision tree logic for candidate outreach - Implement personalization variables in Ashby sequences to track customized messages - Increase sourcing activity on Bookface (Blind) for AI engineer candidates - Start second batch of ClickHouse outreach on Wednesday with 55-55 A/B split (comp in first message vs. comp in second message) - Continue using JuiceBox and automated CSV scraping tool for candidate sourcing - Send strong ClickHouse candidate profile link to Hank for review

Hank - Send back-channel reference request email to Liu about Matt - Ask Chat (from Riviera) if he has connections at Submittable for Matt back-channel - Schedule two additional ClickHouse candidates to interview with Arda - Review strong ClickHouse candidate profile that Zack will send

Arda - Conduct two reference calls for Matt tomorrow (Tuesday) - Schedule third reference call for Matt - Interview two additional ClickHouse candidates that Hank identifies - Document sourcing strategies in the sourcing playbook - Review shared LinkedIn connections with Matt for potential back-channels

Unassigned - None

docs.title: ✅ Decisions Made
docs.text:
• Shift AI engineer recruiting strategy from mass outreach to personalized messaging using AI-powered customization
  • Continue ClickHouse recruiting despite Matt being in pipeline (don't put all eggs in one basket until offer is signed)
  • Run A/B test on second ClickHouse batch: 55 candidates with comp in first message, 55 with comp in second message
  • Move compensation information to second email for half of ClickHouse outreach to test impact
  • Use Bookface (Blind) as additional sourcing channel for AI engineer role
  • Keep all personalized messages within same Ashby sequence for tracking purposes

docs.title: 💬 Talking Points
docs.text:
• AI engineer role response rate is extremely low (0.5% vs. 39% for staff ClickHouse engineer), likely due to market saturation and generic messaging
  • ClickHouse recruiting is performing well with strong response rates, 12 people interviewed, and 111 candidates still to contact
  • Matt's hiring process is moving quickly with reference checks scheduled; team seeking back-channel references through Liu and potentially Chat from Riviera
  • Response rate hypothesis: ClickHouse success was due to highly targeted approach, while AI engineer messages feel generic to candidates who receive many similar outreaches daily
  • Team implementing automated sourcing tools including YC company scraper and JuiceBox CSV extraction

docs.title: 📝 Quick Summary
docs.text: The team reviewed recruiting progress for ClickHouse and AI engineer roles. While ClickHouse recruiting shows strong results (39% response rate, 12 interviews), the AI engineer role is struggling with only 0.5% response rate from 192 outreach sequences. The team decided to pivot strategy by implementing AI-powered personalization using candidates' GitHub and LinkedIn profiles, increasing Bookface sourcing, and continuing A/B testing on compensation messaging. Meanwhile, Matt's hiring process is advancing with reference checks scheduled, and the team is seeking back-channel references while continuing to interview other ClickHouse candidates as a safety measure.

docs.title: ✅ Decisions Made

• The dashboard structure is approved with minor modifications needed (separate panels for different roles)
  • Meeting agenda template will be used as a repeatable format for all Tuesday/Friday syncs
  • Dashboard review will start every meeting, with Hank highlighting any standout metrics immediately
  • Hank will copy and update the agenda template for each subsequent meeting rather than creating from scratch
  • Staff engineer roles will have 3 interview rounds (Zack, Arda, then Zack+Arda together)
  • Backend AI engineer roles will have 4 rounds (Zack, screen 1, screen 2, then Arda)

docs.text:
• Hank created a new recruiting dashboard with 4 panels tracking candidate pipeline metrics, interview stages, and sequence engagement
  • Discussion of interview stage differences between staff engineer and backend AI engineer roles requiring separate tracking
  • Importance of time management and transparency when juggling multiple responsibilities (sourcing, AI tool development, campaign management)
  • AI tools require ongoing monitoring and fine-tuning; they're not "set and forget" solutions
  • Establishing repeatable meeting structure to manage expectations and improve efficiency

docs.title: 📝 Quick Summary
docs.text: Hank presented a new recruiting dashboard and meeting agenda template for the Tuesday/Friday engineering recruiting syncs. Zack provided feedback on dashboard improvements (separating staff and backend AI engineer tracking, adding a blockers section) and emphasized the importance of repeatable processes, time management transparency, and ongoing AI tool monitoring. The team aligned on interview stage structures for different roles and established expectations for consistent meeting formats going forward.

docs.title: Meeting — March 24, 2026 at 02:57 PM
docs.text: Field Value Date March 24, 2026 at 02:57 PM Duration ~26 minutes Speakers Hank

docs.title: 🔑 TL;DR
docs.text: The team is restructuring the AI engineer hiring process to move technical screening to the first interview stage while balancing candidate experience concerns and addressing compensation transparency issues that are affecting response rates.

docs.title: 📋 Action Items
docs.text:
Hank - Update the interview process Notion doc to move Tech Screen 1 to the first interview stage - Remove compensation range from outreach sequences/messages (keep in job description for legal compliance) - Continue sourcing candidates with 2-7 years of experience (avoiding excessively senior candidates) - Prepare 5-minute opening pitch script for engineers conducting first-round technical screens

Zack - Work with Hank to improve the sourcing message prompts - Discuss with Arda and team members about updating Technical Round 2 questions for AI-focused roles - Create new technical interview questions focused on AI-specific topics (prompt tuning, memory, context, beyond basic OpenAI/Anthropic API usage) - Train junior engineers (Diaz, Roy, Daniel) on conducting 30-minute first-round interviews with proper opening pitch

Arda - Collaborate on redesigning Technical Round 2 questions to be more AI-focused

docs.title: ✅ Decisions Made
docs.text:
• Technical Screen 1 will become the first interview stage (moving away from recruiter-first approach)
  • Compensation range will be removed from outreach messages but kept in job descriptions (legal requirement)
  • Tech Screen 1 will be 30 minutes: 10 minutes for company intro/candidate questions, 20 minutes for technical assessment
  • Different interview processes may be applied based on seniority level (more flexibility for senior candidates)
  • Target candidate experience range remains 2-7 years (not pursuing excessively senior engineers)
  • Technical Round 2 needs to be redesigned with AI-specific questions beyond basic API usage

docs.title: 💬 Talking Points
docs.text:
• Concerns about candidate experience when technical screening is the first touchpoint (especially for senior engineers who may reject the process)
  • Compensation expectations are higher than anticipated—multiple candidates indicated the posted range ($220K) is low for "good engineers"
  • Importance of maintaining positive employer branding even with rejected/disqualified candidates (currently receiving positive feedback)
  • Title inflation in the market is driving unrealistic compensation expectations for candidates with limited actual AI/ML experience
  • Need to balance speed of hiring with quality of candidate experience and employer brand protection

docs.title: 📌 Weekly Action List
docs.text:
• Update Notion doc to reflect new interview process flow with Tech Screen 1 first (Owner: Hank, Priority: High)
  • Remove compensation from outreach sequences while keeping in job descriptions (Owner: Hank, Priority: High)
  • Draft and finalize new AI-focused Technical Round 2 questions (Owner: Zack & Arda, Priority: High)
  • Create 5-minute opening pitch script for first-round technical interviewers (Owner: Hank, Priority: Medium)
  • Train junior engineers on conducting 30-minute first interviews with proper structure (Owner: Zack, Priority: Medium)
  • Refine sourcing message prompts to improve response quality (Owner: Zack & Hank, Priority: Medium)

docs.title: ⏰ Deadlines Mentioned
docs.text:
• April 7th — Team travel to Hawaii for company gathering (Hank arrives at 11:30 AM Hawaii time)
  • Within 2 weeks — Hawaii team gathering event
  • This evening or tomorrow — Zack to create new Technical Round 2 questions (self-imposed deadline)

docs.title: ❓ Unresolved Questions
docs.text:
• Should different interview processes be formalized for different seniority levels, or handled case-by-case?
  • Is the current compensation range competitive enough for the market, or does it need adjustment?
  • Can the job description compensation range legally be removed, or must it stay posted?
  • How realistic is it for candidates to complete both algorithm and SQL questions in 20 minutes during Tech Screen 1?
  • What specific AI topics should be tested in Technical Round 2 beyond API usage?

docs.title: 📝 Quick Summary
docs.text: Zack and Hank discussed restructuring the AI engineer hiring process, deciding to move technical screening to the first interview stage to speed up hiring while maintaining candidate experience through a structured 30-minute format. They addressed concerns about compensation transparency after receiving feedback that their posted range appears low to candidates, deciding to remove it from outreach messages but keep it in job descriptions for legal compliance. The team also identified the need to update Technical Round 2 with more AI-specific questions and will work on improving sourcing messages and training junior engineers to conduct first-round interviews effectively.

docs.title: Hiring Pipeline & Junior Candidate Screening — March 24, 2026
docs.text: Field Value Date March 24, 2026 Duration ~13 minutes Speakers Zack, Hank, Arda

docs.title: Analysis
docs.text: The team is shifting their hiring process to implement a technical screen (TechScreen 1) for junior candidates (2 years or less experience) before VP interviews, using a rotation of Daniel, Roy, and Diaz as interviewers.

docs.title: Action Items
docs.text:
Zack - Update outreach messaging to inform candidates about the new technical screen process (first step instead of VP call) - Schedule Henry, Tushar, and Mohana for technical screens - Route junior candidates (2 years or less experience) through TechScreen 1 using the ABCAB rotation (Daniel → Roy → Diaz → Daniel → Roy, repeat) - Continue sourcing candidates from both top-tier and lesser-known companies

Hank - Inform Daniel, Roy, and Diaz about their roles in the technical screening rotation - Continue interviews with more experienced candidates (4+ years)

Arda - Follow up on tomorrow's review schedule changes - Coordinate the call with Ashu Kawat (ML-focused candidate)

docs.title: Decisions Made
docs.text:
• Junior candidates defined as 2 years or less experience
  • Mid-level candidates defined as approximately 3.5-4 years experience
  • Senior candidates defined as 7-8+ years experience
  • New interview process: Junior candidates go through TechScreen 1 first (not directly to VP)
  • Technical screen format: 10 minutes intros/company overview + 20 minutes technical assessment
  • Interviewer rotation established: ABCAB pattern (Daniel, Roy, Diaz, Daniel, Roy - repeat)
  • Candidates with 4+ years experience continue with existing process (direct to Hank)

docs.title: Key Talking Points
docs.text:
• Majority of responding candidates are junior-level (2 years or less), with very few senior candidates (4.5+ years) showing interest
  • Current pipeline has 500 candidates with 400 messages sent out
  • Realistic expectations set: unlikely to hire senior in first half of year, focus on finding strong juniors and possibly a mid-level candidate
  • Strategy includes sourcing from both top-tier companies (LangChain, etc.) and lesser-known companies to find "diamonds in the rough"
  • Technical screening process will help efficiently filter junior candidates before VP-level interviews

docs.title: Deadlines
docs.text:
• Tomorrow — Reviews scheduled (first hour may not happen), call with Ashu Kawat scheduled (Owner: Arda)
  • Tonight/10 minutes — 70 outreach messages scheduled to send (Owner: Zack)
  • Next week — Begin implementing new technical screen process (Owner: Zack)

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: The team discussed restructuring their hiring process to better handle the influx of junior candidates. They decided that candidates with 2 years or less experience will now go through a technical screening round (TechScreen 1) with Daniel, Roy, or Diaz in a rotating pattern before advancing to VP-level interviews. With 500 candidates in the pipeline but mostly junior-level responses, the team set realistic expectations about likely hiring junior or mid-level talent rather than senior engineers in the near term.

docs.title: Interview Process & Compensation Review — March 26, 2026
docs.text: Field Value Date March 26, 2026 Duration ~26 minutes Speakers Zack, Hank, Arda

docs.title: Analysis
docs.text: The team is restructuring the AI engineer hiring process to move technical screening to the first interview stage while balancing candidate experience concerns and addressing compensation transparency issues that are affecting response rates.

docs.title: Action Items
docs.text:
Hank - Update the interview process Notion doc to move Tech Screen 1 to the first interview stage - Remove compensation range from outreach sequences/messages (keep in job description for legal compliance) - Continue sourcing candidates with 2-7 years of experience (avoiding excessively senior candidates) - Prepare 5-minute opening pitch script for engineers conducting first-round technical screens

Zack - Work with Hank to improve the sourcing message prompts - Discuss with Arda and team members about updating Technical Round 2 questions for AI-focused roles - Create new technical interview questions focused on AI-specific topics (prompt tuning, memory, context, beyond basic OpenAI/Anthropic API usage) - Train junior engineers (Diaz, Roy, Daniel) on conducting 30-minute first-round interviews with proper opening pitch

Arda - Collaborate on redesigning Technical Round 2 questions to be more AI-focused

docs.title: Decisions Made
docs.text:
• Technical Screen 1 will become the first interview stage (moving away from recruiter-first approach)
  • Compensation range will be removed from outreach messages but kept in job descriptions (legal requirement)
  • Tech Screen 1 will be 30 minutes: 10 minutes for company intro/candidate questions, 20 minutes for technical assessment
  • Different interview processes may be applied based on seniority level (more flexibility for senior candidates)
  • Target candidate experience range remains 2-7 years (not pursuing excessively senior engineers)
  • Technical Round 2 needs to be redesigned with AI-specific questions beyond basic API usage

docs.title: Key Talking Points
docs.text:
• Concerns about candidate experience when technical screening is the first touchpoint (especially for senior engineers who may reject the process)
  • Compensation expectations are higher than anticipated—multiple candidates indicated the posted range ($220K) is low for "good engineers"
  • Importance of maintaining positive employer branding even with rejected/disqualified candidates (currently receiving positive feedback)
  • Title inflation in the market is driving unrealistic compensation expectations for candidates with limited actual AI/ML experience
  • Need to balance speed of hiring with quality of candidate experience and employer brand protection

docs.title: Deadlines
docs.text:
• April 7th — Team travel to Hawaii for company gathering (Hank arrives at 11:30 AM Hawaii time)
  • Within 2 weeks — Hawaii team gathering event
  • This evening or tomorrow — Zack to create new Technical Round 2 questions (self-imposed deadline)

docs.title: Unresolved Questions
docs.text:
• Should different interview processes be formalized for different seniority levels, or handled case-by-case?
  • Is the current compensation range competitive enough for the market, or does it need adjustment?
  • Can the job description compensation range legally be removed, or must it stay posted?
  • How realistic is it for candidates to complete both algorithm and SQL questions in 20 minutes during Tech Screen 1?
  • What specific AI topics should be tested in Technical Round 2 beyond API usage?

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: Zack and Hank discussed restructuring the AI engineer hiring process, deciding to move technical screening to the first interview stage to speed up hiring while maintaining candidate experience through a structured 30-minute format. They addressed concerns about compensation transparency after receiving feedback that their posted range appears low to candidates, deciding to remove it from outreach messages but keep it in job descriptions for legal compliance. The team also identified the need to update Technical Round 2 with more AI-specific questions and will work on improving sourcing messages and training junior engineers to conduct first-round interviews effectively.

docs.text:
Zack - Source 5 new engineer candidates from LATAM countries (Mexico, Brazil, Puerto Rico) targeting mid-tier companies - Look for candidates at companies like IBM, Accenture, Ernst Young, Deloitte, Visa, MasterCard, American Express, Intuit, or independent contractors - Avoid sourcing from top-tier tech companies (Microsoft, Amazon, AWS, OpenAI, etc.) - Visit San Francisco April 24th weekend to look at apartments with Linda - Notify current rental agency about not renewing lease - Return early from Hawaii trip on April 10th for dad's birthday - Set up pre-tax commuter benefits ($86/month Muni pass) through Rippling - Attend all hands meetings and be in office for on-site visitors

Unassigned - None

docs.title: ✅ Decisions Made
docs.text:

  • Target mid-tier engineers at second-tier companies rather than top-tier tech companies
  • Zack and Linda will move to San Francisco by June 1st

docs.title: 📝 Quick Summary
docs.text: Hank and Zack discussed recruiting strategy and Zack's upcoming move to San Francisco. The key recruiting insight was to shift from targeting overqualified Canadian engineers to mid-level engineers in LATAM countries (Mexico, Brazil, Puerto Rico) who work at second-tier companies like IBM, Accenture, or Visa, as they'd be more likely to join a startup. Hank provided detailed guidance on SF neighborhoods, public transportation, and apartment hunting, with Zack and his girlfriend Linda planning to visit in late April to find housing for a June 1st move-in date.

docs.text: Field Value Date April 3, 2026 Duration ~34 minutes Speakers Zack, Hank

docs.title: Analysis
docs.text: Zack needs to shift recruiting strategy from targeting top-tier engineers at prestigious companies to mid-level engineers at second-tier companies in LATAM (Mexico, Brazil, Puerto Rico), focusing on those at firms like IBM, Accenture, Visa, or independent contractors who would be more likely to join a startup.

docs.title: Action Items
docs.text:
Zack - Source 5 new engineer candidates from LATAM countries (Mexico, Brazil, Puerto Rico) targeting mid-tier companies - Look for candidates at companies like IBM, Accenture, Ernst Young, Deloitte, Visa, MasterCard, American Express, Intuit, or independent contractors - Avoid sourcing from top-tier tech companies (Microsoft, Amazon, AWS, OpenAI, etc.) - Visit San Francisco April 24th weekend to look at apartments with Linda - Notify current rental agency about not renewing lease - Return early from Hawaii trip on April 10th for dad's birthday - Set up pre-tax commuter benefits ($86/month Muni pass) through Rippling - Attend all hands meetings and be in office for on-site visitors

Hank - Provide apartment hunting advice when Zack visits San Francisco - Meet Zack at Hawaii off-site trip (April 7-10)

Linda (Zack's girlfriend) - Interview at bakeries/Dandelion Chocolate while visiting SF in late April - Complete move to San Francisco by June 1st

docs.title: Decisions Made
docs.text:
• Recruiting strategy will shift from Canada to LATAM (Mexico, Brazil, Puerto Rico)
  • Target mid-tier engineers at second-tier companies rather than top-tier tech companies
  • Zack and Linda will move to San Francisco by June 1st
  • Zack will leave current apartment by May 31st (last rent payment May 1st)
  • Zack will use pre-tax commuter benefits for public transportation

docs.title: Key Talking Points
docs.text:
• Recruiting strategy pivot: Moving away from Canadian candidates who are overqualified and targeting LATAM engineers at mid-tier companies who would be more likely to join a startup
  • San Francisco relocation planning: Zack and Linda's move timeline, apartment hunting in late April, and neighborhood options (Mission District, Bayview, Richmond, etc.)
  • Public transportation guidance: Hank provided detailed overview of SF Muni system, light rail, buses, and commuter benefits
  • Work-life balance in SF: Discussion of Bay Area activities, arts & wine festivals, hiking opportunities, and better weather compared to desert climate
  • Office attendance expectations: Zack plans to be in office for on-site visitors, all hands meetings (Mondays), and key meeting days (Tuesdays/Fridays)

docs.title: Deadlines
docs.text:
• April 7-10 — Hawaii off-site trip, Zack attending (leaving early April 10th for dad's birthday)
  • April 24 (approx.) — Zack and Linda visit SF to look at apartments
  • May 1 — Last rent payment at current apartment (Zack)
  • May 31 — Move out of current apartment (Zack and Linda)
  • June 1 — Target move-in date for SF apartment (Zack and Linda)
