docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
The FORGE brain-and-classifier system routes Discord messages from natural language into executable actions, orchestrating a dual-layer pipeline:

  1. Classifier: models rapidly categorizes messages into ~300 action types with confidence scoring
  2. Brain: models interprets complex requests, loops up to 10 times over tool use, and orchestrates multi-step tasks
  3. Router: Legacy pattern-match fallback (disabled when BRAIN_FIRST=1, the default)

The architecture prioritizes autonomy: the brain can read Discord channels, post messages, queue tasks for the Copilot agent, and manage its own context state through the brain-state immutable dataclass.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
The classifier operates as a lightweight intent gatekeeper before expensive Claude operations. It answers a single question: "What does the user want to do?" The response is structured JSON with an action field (one of ~300 defined action types) and a confidence score (0.0–1.0).

Model: models (fastest tier) Speed: ~200ms per classification Cost: ~150 tokens per call Caching: Automatic prompt caching for system messages > 1024 chars

docs.title: Action Types
docs.text:
Actions are defined in core/classifier_prompt.py and cover six categories:

Category Example Actions Count Browser browser_open, browser_screenshot, browser_close, browser_read 6 System pc_lock, pc_stats, pc_volume, pc_shutdown, pc_restart 8 Media media_play_pause, media_next, media_mute 5 Email email_inbox, email_send, email_search, email_mass_delete 12 Calendar calendar_add, calendar_update, calendar_reschedule 7 Sourcing prism_score, outreach_draft, candidate_search 15+ Other Settings, file ops, monitoring, scheduling, recording, etc. ~250

docs.title: Classification Process
docs.text:
When a message arrives:

  1. Message received → Discord fires on_message event in bot.py
  2. Conversational guard → Skip if message is simple yes/no/thanks (no point in classifying)
  3. Pre-classifier intercepts → Hardcoded triggers for frequently-misrouted actions like rc status, nba picks
  4. Classifier call → _classify_action(text) calls models with:
  5. Static system prompt: ~470 action definitions
  6. Dynamic examples: Recent correct classifications from feedback system
  7. Sequence hints: If the user's last action was X, what should they do next?
  8. Context: Last 4 messages in the conversation
  9. Result parsing → JSON extraction from Haiku response
  10. Confidence check → If confidence < 0.5, escalate to brain instead
  11. Dispatch → Route to appropriate cogs or brain

docs.title: Confidence System
docs.text:
The classifier returns a confidence float (0.0–1.0). This value gates:

  • High confidence (>0.8): Execute the action immediately via the cog system
  • Medium confidence (0.5–0.8): Optionally log for retraining
  • Low confidence (<0.5): Escalate to the brain (more expensive, but handles ambiguity)

Example: "should I email this person?" has low confidence because it's a yes/no question requiring reasoning, not action. The brain takes over.

docs.title: Feedback System
docs.text:
Failed classifications are logged and fed back into future classifications via the feedback system. This creates a self-correcting loop:

# From core/classifier_feedback.py
dynamic_examples = await classifier_feedback.get_dynamic_examples(limit=10)
# Returns: "User said 'check my email', classifier predicted email_inbox ✓"

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
The FORGE brain is a persistent Claude Sonnet agent that handles:

  • Complex reasoning and multi-step tasks
  • Tool use loops (up to 10 iterations)
  • Discord channel reading and writing
  • Copilot task queuing
  • Conversation state persistence

Model: models Max iterations: 10 (tool use loop) Token budget: Configurable per conversation Cost: ~$0.003 per call (vs. ~$0.0001 for classifier)

docs.title: Tool Use Loop (10 Iterations)
docs.text:
The brain's power comes from tool use: Claude can call functions, inspect results, and loop. The ask() method in core/brain.py implements a tool-use loop:

graph LR
    A[Brain analyzes] --> B{Needs tool?}
    B -->|Yes| C[Call web_search]
    C --> D[Receive results]
    D --> B
    B -->|Yes| E[Call fetch_url]
    E --> F[Receive content]
    F --> B
    B -->|Yes| G[Call forge_write_channel]
    G --> H[Task complete]
    B -->|No| H

Each iteration:

  1. Claude receives messages + available tools
  2. Claude analyzes and decides: "Do I need a tool?"
  3. If stop_reason == "tool_use", execute all tool calls
  4. Append tool results to messages
  5. Loop back (up to 10 times)
  6. If stop_reason == "end_turn", return text response

docs.title: Available Tools
docs.text: The brain has access to three tool sets:

docs.title: 1. FORGE Internal Tools (core/forge_tools.py)
docs.text:
Always available. Core Discord and task control:

Tool Purpose forge_read_channel Read recent messages from any Discord channel forge_write_channel Post a message to Discord forge_copilot_task Queue a coding task for the Copilot agent forge_edit_message Edit a previously sent message forge_delete_message Delete a message forge_react Add emoji reaction to a message forge_create_thread Create a thread in a channel

docs.title: 2. Brain Tools (core/brain_tools.py)
docs.text:
Research and knowledge access:

Tool Purpose web_search Search the web (Serper API) fetch_url Extract content from a URL search_knowledge Search FORGE's internal ChromaDB knowledge base search_candidates Query the candidate evaluation database add_candidate Add a new candidate to tracking

docs.title: 3. MCP Tools (via mcp_manager)
docs.text:
Optional, dynamically loaded based on available MCP servers. Examples:

  • GitHub API tools
  • Anthropic API tools
  • Email/calendar tools
  • Other integrations

docs.title: Brain State (core/brain_state.py)
docs.text:
Each brain conversation is immutable:

@dataclass(frozen=True)
class BrainConversation:
    thread_id: str              # Unique ID for this conversation
    user_id: str                # Discord user ID
    channel_id: int             # Discord channel ID
    messages: tuple[dict, ...]  # Full message history
    active_tools: frozenset[str]  # Tools available in this conversation
    cost_accumulated: float     # Total API spend
    facts_injected: tuple[str, ...]  # Additional context injected
    correlation_id: str         # Trace ID for observability
    created_at: str            # ISO 8601 timestamp
    updated_at: str            # Last modification time

All updates return new frozen instances (immutable pattern):

conv = conv.with_message({"role": "user", "content": "..."})
conv = conv.with_cost(0.005)  # Add $0.005 to cost

Persisted to SQLite (data/forge.db, table brain_conversations).

docs.title: Token Budget & Caching
docs.text:
The brain tracks token usage and implements:

  1. Prompt caching: System messages > 1024 chars use Anthropic's ephemeral cache, saving 90% of tokens on repeated calls
  2. Response caching: LRU cache (max 100 responses) for graceful degradation when API fails
  3. Token budgeting: Via modules/token_budget.py, tracks spend per model/source
  4. Circuit breaker: If API fails repeatedly, return cached responses instead of hammering the API

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
When BRAIN_FIRST=0 (disabled by default), FORGE uses the legacy classifier-router pattern defined in core/classifier_router.py.

Status: Deprecated in favor of brain-first architecture.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
1. Classify via _classify_action() → returns {action, confidence}
  2. Route via _try_action_intent() → dispatch to cog or brain
  3. Execute → cog handles the action synchronously or queues async work

docs.title: When It's Still Used
docs.text:
• Fallback if BRAIN_FIRST=0 in core/config.py
  • Pre-classifier keyword intercepts (hardcoded rules for commonly-misrouted actions)
  • High-confidence classifications route directly to cogs (faster)

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
The forge_multi_action action handles compound requests: when the user asks for multiple things at once.

Example: "Check the talent channel, draft an outreach message, and post it to Slack."

docs.title: Flow
docs.text:
1. User message arrives
  2. Classifier tags it as forge_multi_action
  3. Brain is invoked with Multi-Action Tools
  4. Brain reads channels, composes messages, posts results
  5. All three tasks happen in a single brain call

docs.title: Multi-Action Tools
docs.text:
Subset of FORGE tools, optimized for Discord orchestration:

  • forge_read_channel — read channel history
  • forge_write_channel — post to Discord
  • forge_copilot_task — queue code tasks
  • forge_edit_message — edit sent messages
  • forge_delete_message — clean up
  • forge_react — react with emojis
  • forge_create_thread — start threads

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: When FORGE starts (bot.py), the core/bootstrap.py initializes all modules in dependency order. Critical failures abort the boot; non-critical failures are logged and skipped.

docs.title: Phase 1: Core Infrastructure
docs.text:
graph TD
    A["1. FORGE Monitor<br/>Catches and logs errors"] --> B["2. Process Monitor<br/>Tracks memory, CPU, health"]
    B --> C["3. Database CRITICAL<br/>Create tables, init schema"]
    C --> D{Success?}
    D -->|No| E["Abort"]
    D -->|Yes| F["Continue"]

docs.title: Phase 2: JD/Sourcing Filters
docs.text:
graph TD
    A["4. JD Manager<br/>Load job descriptions"] --> B["5. Sourcing Filters<br/>Set active filters in memory"]

docs.title: Phase 3: Classifiers & Models
docs.text:
graph TD
    A["6. Intent Registry<br/>Load ~300 actions"] --> B["7. Classifier Feedback<br/>Init feedback system"]

docs.title: Phase 4: Persistence & State
docs.text:
graph TD
    A["8. Brain State<br/>Load conversations"] --> B["9. Skill Manager<br/>Load user skills"]

docs.title: Phase 5: Skills & Integrations
docs.text:
graph TD
    A["10. MCP Manager<br/>Connect to MCPs"] --> B["11. Skills Loader<br/>Load from core/skills/"]
    B --> C["12. Cog Loader<br/>Load all cogs"]

docs.title: Phase 6: Scheduled Tasks
docs.text:
graph TD
    A["13. Autonomy Loop<br/>Every 6 hours"] --> B["14. Overnight Engine<br/>8 PM daily"]
    B --> C["15. Sports Pipeline<br/>10 AM daily"]

docs.title: Critical vs. Non-Critical
docs.text:
Critical (fail = abort boot): - Database initialization - Config loading

Non-critical (fail = log and continue): - Skill loading - Cog loading - MCP connections - Scheduled tasks

docs.title: Example: Failed Skill Load
docs.text:
Module initialize phase... [OK]
Loading skills from core/skills/...
  - skill_search_candidates.py ... [OK]
  - skill_email_draft.py ... [FAIL] (import error)
  - skill_calendar.py ... [OK]
  Database still initialized, other skills work.
  Error logged to system monitor.
  Boot continues.

docs.title: Complete Path from Discord to Response
docs.text:
graph TD
    A[User sends message] --> B[discord.py on_message event]
    B --> C[Parse message]
    C --> D{BRAIN_FIRST=1?}
    D -->|Yes| E[Call brain.ask directly]
    D -->|No| F[Call try_action_intent]
    F --> G[Classify message]
    G --> H[Dispatch to cog or brain]
    E --> I[Brain reads/writes Discord]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Post response to Discord]
    J --> K[Log action to database]

docs.title: Brain-First Flow (Default)
docs.text:
# From bot.py:on_message()
if BRAIN_FIRST:
    response = await brain.ask(
        prompt=message.content,
        system=BRAIN_SYSTEM_PROMPT,
        tools=FORGE_TOOLS + MCP_TOOLS,
        tool_handler=tool_handler,
    )
    await message.reply(response)

The brain automatically: - Reads/writes Discord - Calls tools as needed - Loops up to 10 times - Returns final response

docs.title: Legacy Flow (Disabled by Default)
docs.text:
# From classifier_router.py:try_action_intent()
intent = await _classify_action(message.content)
# Returns: {"action": "email_send", "confidence": 0.87}

if intent["confidence"] > 0.8:
    # Fast-path to cog
    cog = dispatch_to_cog(intent["action"])
    await cog.execute(message, intent)
else:
    # Escalate to brain
    await brain.ask(message.content, ...)

docs.title: Key Files
docs.text: File Lines Purpose core/brain.py ~350 Main Claude API wrapper, tool-use loop (10 iterations), token tracking, response caching core/classifier_prompt.py ~470 Static prompt with ~300 action definitions and examples core/classifier_router.py ~600 Legacy routing (classifier → intent → cog or brain) — deprecated core/brain_tools.py ~250 Tool definitions for research, knowledge search, candidate ops core/forge_tools.py ~200 Tool definitions for Discord control (read/write/react/thread) core/multi_action.py ~150 Specialized tools for compound requests (forge_multi_action) core/brain_state.py ~350 Immutable brain conversation dataclass + SQLite persistence core/bootstrap.py ~200+ Module initialization sequence, dependency order bot.py ~6700 Main Discord bot entry point, on_message dispatch, cog loading

docs.title: Related Systems
docs.text:
• core/config.py — Haiku/Sonnet/Opus configuration
  • Cogs — Modular action handlers (email, calendar, browser, etc.)
  • Skills — Saved user routines
  • MCP Manager — Integration layer for external tools
  • ChromaDB — Semantic knowledge base (brain search)
  • Copilot Agent — Persistent coding assistant (queued by brain)

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Classifier Feedback System — Self-correcting classifier via examples
  • Routing Optimizer — Dynamic examples for faster routing
  • Pipeline Graph — StateGraph runner for multi-phase workflows
  • Resilience Patterns — Circuit breaker, token budgeting, graceful degradation

docs.title: Copilot & Agent Lab
docs.text:
3 min read · Last updated April 04, 2026 · 579 words

FORGE's autonomous coding and sourcing research systems.

docs.title: Copilot: Persistent Claude Code Pipe
docs.text: FORGE Copilot is a fully autonomous Claude Code agent running as a dedicated persistent process with bidirectional stdin/stdout streaming. Unlike traditional API calls, Copilot stays alive between Discord messages—near-instant after first boot.

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text:
• One persistent Claude Code CLI process managed by FORGE
  • Bidirectional JSON streaming: user messages written to stdin, responses streamed from stdout
  • Max subscription OAuth: strips API key, uses stored credentials
  • No claude-agent-sdk: avoids Pydantic V1/V2 conflicts with ChromaDB in the same process
  • Auto-recovery: health monitor (30s interval) restarts dead processes transparently
  • Message queue: concurrent messages queued automatically, no blocking

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
SessionFile: data/copilot_session.json (OAuth state)
PIDFile: data/copilot_pid.json (process tracking)
MCPFile: data/copilot_mcp.json (registered MCPs)
ActiveTask: data/copilot_active_task.json (current work)

Constants: - MAX_MSG_LEN: 1900 chars (Discord limit with margin) - MAX_TURNS: 30 per response - PIPE_IDLE_TIMEOUT: 600s (stop idle process) - HEALTH_CHECK_INTERVAL: 30s - QUEUE_MAX_SIZE: 20 messages

docs.title: System Prompt Rules
docs.text: Copilot knows: - Full FORGE access (Read, Write, Edit, Bash, Grep, Glob) - Root: C:\Users\User\FORGE\ (absolute paths mandatory) - Main: bot.py (~7300 lines), modules, cogs, config - Critical: concise output (2000 char Discord limit), never echo context - Jump straight to ACTION—first line should be what it's doing, not what it knows - Match Zack's casual, straight-to-point style

docs.title: Copilot Awareness
docs.text:
The copilot_awareness.py module detects when a message needs code changes:

  • Feature requests: "add new command"
  • Bug fixes: "fix the issue with"
  • Behavior changes: "make forge respond"
  • Integration: "hook up X API"
  • Code-specific: "refactor", "rewrite", ".py"

If a request needs code work, FORGE suggests routing to Copilot automatically.

docs.title: Agent Lab: Sourcing Debate System
docs.text: Agent Lab is two autonomous AI agents (Scout 🔍 and Analyst 📊) debating sourcing strategies and candidate pipeline approaches. Runs hourly 8am-11pm in the 💡-lab channel.

docs.title: Scout vs Analyst
docs.text: Role Focus Style Scout 🔍 Creative sourcing, unconventional channels Brainstormer, experimenter Analyst 📊 Data-driven yield/cost/ROI Evaluator, optimizer

docs.title: Session Types
docs.text:
1. JD Breakdown (📋): Sourcing angles, keywords, Boolean strings, talent communities
  2. Channel Strategy (📡): Compare Slack, GitHub, LinkedIn X-ray, Discord, Reddit, StackOverflow
  3. Outreach Playbook (✉️): Messaging strategies, personalization at scale, timing/tone
  4. Pipeline Review (📊): Bottleneck analysis, conversion rates, source quality
  5. Competitive Sourcing (🏢): Target company strategies, org mapping, signal detection
  6. Wild Card (🤯): Unconventional approaches (commit patterns, blog analysis, patent filings)
  7. VPS Autonomy (🖥️): FORGE 24/7 independence—monitoring, scheduling, self-healing

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
• Separate API key: isolates costs from main FORGE usage
  • Default rounds: 6, max 12
  • Daily limit: 30 sessions
  • Cooldown: 30 minutes between sessions
  • Model: Haiku (fast, cost-effective)

docs.title: Interactive Mode
docs.text:
Zack can join a session and steer the agents in real-time. Reply to the agents' messages to influence their analysis.

"brainstorm 5 outreach playbooks"
→ Scout & Analyst debate 5 different outreach strategies
→ Reply with feedback to steer them

"done" → wraps up the session

docs.title: Key Files
docs.text:
File Purpose modules/copilot.py Copilot process manager, health monitor, JSON streaming core/copilot_awareness.py Detects code-change requests, auto-routes to Copilot core/copilot_context.py Assembles rich context (goals, handoff, decisions, failures) modules/agent_lab.py Scout & Analyst session orchestration, batch mode cogs/agent_lab.py Discord action handlers (brainstorm, interactive, history)

See also:

docs.title: Discord Bot Infrastructure
docs.text:
13 min read · Last updated April 04, 2026 · 2,599 words

FORGE is a sophisticated Discord bot built on discord.py that orchestrates AI-powered workflows, sourcing pipelines, and real-time progress tracking. This article covers the core architectural components that make FORGE function across 6,698 lines of bot logic, 34 cogs, and 38+ modules.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
FORGE operates on a dispatcher + event-driven architecture:

  • Classifier: Routes natural language → ~300 action types
  • Cog System: 34 specialized cogs handle domain-specific logic (sourcing, lab, admin, views, etc.)
  • Event Bus: Lightweight pub/sub for cross-module causation tracking
  • Middleware Chain: Pre/post-processing around Claude API calls
  • Progress Tracker: Real-time Discord message updates for long-running tasks
  • Channel Manager: Unified interface for reading/writing Discord messages

This design separates concerns while maintaining a single message handler in bot.py that stays clean and testable.

docs.title: Bot Initialization & Message Routing
docs.text: File: bot.py

docs.title: Duplicate Instance Guard
docs.text:
FORGE prevents two bot.py processes from running simultaneously using PID tracking:

bot_pid.json → stores PID + startup timestamp
_check_bot_duplicate() → verifies process is still alive before allowing startup

The guard uses psutil to validate: 1. Process exists and is Python 2. Process is running bot.py (not a recycled PID)

docs.title: Core Message Handler
docs.text:
The main on_message event processes every Discord message through:

  1. Ignore checks: Skip bot messages, private DMs, empty content
  2. Relay check: If Claude-app relay is active, intercept and forward
  3. Classifier: Natural language → action type
  4. Dispatcher: Action type → cog handler via HANDLED_ACTIONS
  5. Brain call (optional): Invoke Claude for multi-action workflows
  6. Cog execution: Async task execution with error handling

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
File: core/config.py

Environment-based settings include:

DISCORD_BOT_TOKEN           # Bot authentication
DISCORD_GUILD_ID            # Primary guild
DISCORD_CHANNEL_ID          # Default channel (⚡-forge)
MODELS                      # Haiku, Sonnet, Opus endpoints
API_KEYS                    # Anthropic, Tavily, Exa, Notte, etc.
LAB_CHANNEL_ID              # 💡-lab (autonomous brainstorming)
SCOUT_CHANNEL_ID            # Sourcing scout operations
MONITOR_CHANNEL_ID          # 📡-monitor (error detection)
COPILOT_CHANNEL_ID          # 🎮-copilot (Claude↔Discord bridge)

All config is lazy-loaded to support hot-reload of values.

docs.title: Cog System (HANDLED_ACTIONS)
docs.text:
File: cogs/init.py (typically minimal)

Each cog is a commands.Cog subclass that:

  1. Defines one or more actions via HANDLED_ACTIONS dict
  2. Implements handle_action(action, **kwargs) async method
  3. Lives in cogs/ directory and is auto-loaded at startup

docs.title: HANDLED_ACTIONS Pattern
docs.text:
# In a cog:
HANDLED_ACTIONS = {
    "retrieve_jd": {
        "description": "Fetch job description from Ashby",
        "params": ["job_id"],
    },
    "evaluate_candidate": {
        "description": "Run PRISM evaluation",
        "params": ["profile_text", "score_threshold"],
    },
}

async def handle_action(self, action: str, **kwargs):
    if action == "retrieve_jd":
        return await self._fetch_jd(kwargs["job_id"])
    elif action == "evaluate_candidate":
        return await self._prism_evaluate(kwargs["profile_text"], kwargs["score_threshold"])

docs.title: Built-in Cogs
docs.text: Cog Purpose Key Actions admin.py Owner-only commands reload, reload_all, sync_commands, cogs views.py Persistent UI panels ConfirmView, DestructiveConfirmView, ScoutControlView sourcing_cmds.py Candidate sourcing search_linkedin, enrich_profile, score_candidate lab.py Autonomous brainstorming agent_run, agent_status, agent_output tts_actions.py Text-to-speech pipeline speak, stream_audio ~30 more Specialized domains Valorant, PrizePicks, enrichment, monitoring, etc.

docs.title: Hot-Reload
docs.text:
Admin commands allow live cog reloading without restarting the bot:

/reload sourcing_cmds  # Reloads just sourcing_cmds cog
/reload_all            # Reloads all cogs
/sync_commands         # Re-syncs slash commands to Discord

docs.title: Event Bus
docs.text:
File: core/event_bus.py — Holy Grail #9

A lightweight async pub/sub system for cross-module causation tracking.

docs.title: Key Features
docs.text:
• Typed Events: Dataclass-based events with correlation IDs
  • Causation Chains: Parent event IDs track call hierarchy
  • SQLite Persistence: Events survive crashes for analytics
  • Bounded Queue: Prevents backlog (MAX_QUEUE = 500)
  • Subscriber Isolation: One bad handler doesn't crash others

docs.title: Event Dataclass
docs.text:
@dataclass
class Event:
    event_type: str              # "candidate_found", "brain_call_completed", etc.
    data: dict                   # Payload
    source_module: str           # "pipeline_scout", "middleware.cost_tracking"
    event_id: str                # Auto-generated UUID
    parent_event_id: Optional[str]   # Links to parent (causation)
    correlation_id: str          # Groups related events
    timestamp: str               # ISO 8601

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
from core.event_bus import event_bus

# Emit an event
event_id = await event_bus.emit(
    "candidate_found",
    {"name": "Jane", "source": "github", "score": 4.2},
    source_module="pipeline_scout",
    parent_event_id=strategy_event_id,
)

# Subscribe to events
@event_bus.on("candidate_found")
async def handle_candidate(event):
    print(f"New candidate: {event.data['name']}")

# Query causation chain (root → leaf)
chain = await event_bus.get_chain(event_id)

# Get all events in a correlation group
group = await event_bus.get_correlation_group(correlation_id)

# Get descendants (recursive)
descendants = await event_bus.get_descendants(event_id)

docs.title: Middleware Integration
docs.text:
The event bus is automatically wired into #Middleware Chain:

  • EventBusMiddleware: Emits brain_call_started / brain_call_completed events
  • CostTrackingMiddleware: Emits brain_cost_tracked with token counts and pricing

docs.title: Middleware Chain
docs.text:
File: core/middleware_chain.py

Inspired by Deer-Flow, adapted for FORGE's immutable-first design.

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text:
Middlewares are applied in onion model:

graph LR
    A["Input"] --> B["mw[0]"]
    B --> C["mw[1]"]
    C --> D["mw[2]"]
    D --> E["Claude API"]
    E --> F["mw[2]"]
    F --> G["mw[1]"]
    G --> H["mw[0]"]
    H --> I["Output"]

docs.title: Core Data Types
docs.text:
@dataclass(frozen=True)
class BrainContext:
    """Immutable snapshot before Claude API call."""
    messages: tuple[dict, ...]
    system_prompt: str
    user_id: str
    channel_id: int
    model: str
    tools: tuple[dict, ...]
    correlation_id: str
    extra: dict

@dataclass(frozen=True)
class BrainResponse:
    """Immutable snapshot after Claude API call."""
    content: str
    tool_calls: tuple[dict, ...]
    usage: dict
    cost: float
    stop_reason: str

docs.title: EventBusMiddleware
docs.text:
Emits lifecycle events around Claude calls:

  • brain_call_started: Before API call (includes message count, tool count)
  • brain_call_completed: After API call (includes duration, stop_reason, tool_call_count)

Tracks start times per correlation_id to calculate duration.

docs.title: CostTrackingMiddleware
docs.text:
Logs cost and usage tokens:

Brain call cost: $0.002345 (in=512, out=128, model=claude-sonnet-4-5)

Emits brain_cost_tracked event for centralized cost aggregation.

docs.title: SubagentLimitMiddleware
docs.text:
Prevents runaway forge_copilot_task tool spawning:

  • Default: max 3 copilot calls per brain response
  • Excess calls are truncated with a warning
  • Helps prevent token explosion from nested subagent spawning

docs.title: Usage in FORGE
docs.text:
from core.middleware_chain import default_chain, BrainContext, BrainResponse

chain = default_chain()

# Before call
ctx = BrainContext(
    messages=tuple(messages),
    system_prompt=system,
    user_id=str(author.id),
    channel_id=channel.id,
    model=model_id,
    tools=tuple(tools),
    correlation_id=correlation_id,
)
ctx = await chain.run_before(ctx)

# Make Claude API call
response = await client.messages.create(...)

# After call
resp = BrainResponse(
    content=response.content[0].text,
    tool_calls=tuple(tool_dicts),
    usage={"input_tokens": response.usage.input_tokens, ...},
    cost=calculated_cost,
    stop_reason=response.stop_reason,
)
resp = await chain.run_after(ctx, resp)

# Wrap tool execution
result = await chain.run_tool("forge_read_channel", args, base_handler)

docs.title: Progress Tracking
docs.text:
File: core/progress.py

Universal progress display for long-running operations. Shows real-time status in Discord via throttled message edits.

docs.title: ProgressTracker
docs.text:
tracker = ProgressTracker(
    channel=channel,
    title="PRISM Evaluation",
    edit_interval=1.5,  # Throttle edits to avoid rate limits
    show_elapsed=True,
    show_spinner=True,
    max_display_steps=8,
)

async with tracker:
    await tracker.step("Loading JD...")
    # ... do work ...

    await tracker.step("Evaluating candidate...")
    await tracker.substep("Checking tech stack")
    # ... do work ...

    await tracker.complete("Score: 4.2/5")

docs.title: Display Format
docs.text:
🔄 **PRISM Evaluation**
✅ Loading JD...
✅ Fetching GitHub history...
⠋ Evaluating candidate...
   └ Checking tech stack
⏱️ 3.2s

When finished:

✅ **PRISM Evaluation**
✅ Loading JD...
✅ Evaluating candidate...

Score: 4.2/5
⏱️ 12.4s

docs.title: Streaming Support
docs.text:
For Claude responses, use on_token callback:

tracker = ProgressTracker(channel, title="Thinking")
async with tracker:
    response = await brain.ask_stream(
        messages=messages,
        on_token=tracker.stream_text,
    )

Shows last ~200 chars of streaming text as preview.

docs.text:
For optional progress tracking, use NoOpTracker:

from core.progress import get_tracker

async def long_operation(tracker=None):
    tracker = get_tracker(tracker)
    await tracker.step("Starting...")

docs.title: Discord Channel Management
docs.text:
File: modules/discord_channels.py

Unified interface for reading/writing Discord messages across any channel FORGE can access.

docs.title: Channel Resolution
docs.text:
from modules.discord_channels import resolve_channel

# Resolve by ID
ch = await resolve_channel(bot, 1473339250396364900)

# Resolve by name (with emoji normalization)
ch = await resolve_channel(bot, "⚡-forge")      # → "forge"
ch = await resolve_channel(bot, "forge")        # → exact match

# Resolve by alias
ch = await resolve_channel(bot, "lab")          # → LAB_CHANNEL_ID
ch = await resolve_channel(bot, "monitor")      # → MONITOR_CHANNEL_ID

Priority: numeric ID → config alias → fuzzy guild scan.

docs.title: Claude↔Discord Relay
docs.text:
File: modules/relay.py

Enables Claude (from the Claude desktop app) to post to #copilot and receive Zack's replies via Discord MCP without spawning the copilot CLI.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
1. Claude posts to #copilot via Discord MCP
  2. FORGE detects Claude² bot message
  3. If message has attention prefix, FORGE DMs Zack
  4. Zack replies in #copilot (from phone, etc.)
  5. Claude reads reply via Discord MCP (no CLI spawn)
  6. Session auto-times out after 15m idle

docs.title: Attention Prefixes (trigger DM)
docs.text:
🔔 Need input: [question]
❓ Should we do X?
⚠️ Found a problem: [details]
❌ Build failed
✅ Task complete
🏁 Session done

docs.title: Status Prefixes (no DM)
docs.text:
📋 Working on X...
🔧 Editing file Y
📝 Found 3 issues
⏳ In progress

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
from modules.relay import handle_relay_message, get_relay_status

# In on_message handler
if await handle_relay_message(message):
    return  # Message handled, skip copilot CLI

# Get relay status
status = get_relay_status()
# "🟢 **Relay active** (5m) — 12 messages"
# "⚫ **Relay idle** — post to #copilot to start"

docs.title: Session Control
docs.text:
In #copilot, Zack can:

end relay        # End relay, restore copilot CLI
stop relay       # (alias)
copilot mode     # (alias)

docs.title: Admin Commands
docs.text:
File: cogs/admin.py

Owner-only commands (ZACK_ID) for managing FORGE.

docs.title: Commands
docs.text: Command Purpose /reload {cog} Hot-reload a single cog /reload_all Hot-reload all cogs /sync_commands Re-sync slash commands to Discord /cogs List loaded cogs and status

docs.title: Implementation
docs.text:
@app_commands.command(name="reload")
async def cmd_reload(self, interaction: discord.Interaction, cog: str):
    if not self._is_owner(interaction):
        await interaction.response.send_message("Owner only.", ephemeral=True)
        return

    ext = f"cogs.{cog}"
    try:
        await self.bot.reload_extension(ext)
        await interaction.response.send_message(f"Reloaded `{ext}`.")
    except Exception as e:
        await interaction.response.send_message(f"Failed: {e}")

All admin commands are owner-guarded via _is_owner() check.

docs.title: Interactive Views
docs.text:
File: cogs/views.py

Persistent button-based UI panels using discord.py views.

docs.title: ConfirmView
docs.text:
Simple yes/no confirmation:

view = ConfirmView(author_id=ZACK_ID, timeout=60)
msg = await channel.send("Proceed with enrichment?", view=view)

if await view.wait():  # Wait for interaction
    if view.value:
        await channel.send("✅ Confirmed")
    else:
        await channel.send("❌ Cancelled")

docs.title: DestructiveConfirmView
docs.text:
Confirmation with countdown timer for destructive actions:

view = DestructiveConfirmView("Restart FORGE", timeout=12)
msg = await channel.send("⚠️ **Restart FORGE?** This will interrupt tasks.", view=view)
await view.start_countdown(msg)

if await view.wait():
    if view.value:
        # User confirmed after countdown
    else:
        # Timed out or cancelled

Countdown runs 10 → 8 → 6 → 4 → 2 seconds on the button label.

docs.title: ScoutControlView
docs.text:
Sourcing scout operations (run, stats, autopilot):

  • Button-based control for pipeline operations
  • Persistent across restarts (custom_id + timeout=None)
  • Integrates with #Progress Tracking for status updates

docs.title: Implementation Pattern
docs.text:
class MyView(discord.ui.View):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__(timeout=None)  # Persistent
        self.value = None

    @discord.ui.button(label="Confirm", style=discord.ButtonStyle.green)
    async def confirm_btn(self, interaction: discord.Interaction, button: discord.ui.Button):
        if interaction.user.id != ZACK_ID:
            await interaction.response.send_message("Not authorized", ephemeral=True)
            return

        self.value = True
        self.stop()
        await interaction.response.edit_message(view=None)

All views use custom_id for persistent message handling across bot restarts.

docs.title: Shared Utilities
docs.text: File: cogs/_utils.py

docs.title: bot_main()
docs.text:
Access the main bot module:

_m = bot_main()
result = await _m.search_candidate_db(query=query)

Useful in cogs that need to call bot.py-defined functions.

docs.title: send_long()
docs.text:
Split long messages at newlines to stay under Discord 2000-char limit:

await send_long(channel, very_long_text)

Preserves first message for reaction tracking.

docs.title: send_long_message()
docs.text:
Interaction-aware version:

await send_long_message(interaction, very_long_text, suppress_embeds=True)

docs.title: is_confirm_yes()
docs.text:
Parse user confirmation response:

user_reply = "yeah go"
if is_confirm_yes(user_reply):
    # Proceed

Recognizes: yes, y, run, ok, sure, go, yep, yeah, and variations.

docs.title: Key Files Reference
docs.text: File Purpose Key Classes/Functions bot.py Main entry point _check_bot_duplicate(), on_message, cog loading core/config.py Environment configuration DISCORD_BOT_TOKEN, MODELS, LAB_CHANNEL_ID, etc. core/event_bus.py Pub/sub event system Event, CrossModuleEventBus, event_bus singleton core/middleware_chain.py Claude call pre/post-processing BrainContext, BrainResponse, MiddlewareChain, default middlewares core/progress.py Real-time Discord progress display ProgressTracker, NoOpTracker, get_tracker() modules/discord_channels.py Channel operations resolve_channel(), read_channel_messages(), write_channel_message(), etc. modules/relay.py Claude↔Discord relay handle_relay_message(), get_relay_status() cogs/init.py Cog auto-loader Typically minimal; cogs discovered from cogs/ directory cogs/admin.py Admin commands /reload, /reload_all, /sync_commands, /cogs cogs/views.py Interactive UI panels ConfirmView, DestructiveConfirmView, ScoutControlView cogs/_utils.py Shared cog utilities bot_main(), send_long(), is_confirm_yes()

docs.title: Architecture Diagram
docs.text:
graph TD
    A["Discord Message"] --> B["bot.py: on_message"]
    B --> C["Ignore checks"]
    C --> D["Relay check"]
    D --> E["Classifier<br/>NLP to action"]
    E --> F["HANDLED_ACTIONS<br/>dispatcher"]
    F --> G["Cog handler"]
    G --> H["Progress tracking<br/>ProgressTracker"]
    G --> I["Claude API calls<br/>Middleware chain"]
    I --> I1["EventBusMiddleware"]
    I --> I2["CostTrackingMiddleware"]
    I --> I3["SubagentLimitMiddleware"]
    G --> J["Channel I/O<br/>discord_channels"]
    K["Event Bus<br/>cross-module pub/sub"] -.->|brain_call_started| L["100+ event types"]
    K -.->|brain_call_completed| L
    K -.->|brain_cost_tracked| L
    K -.->|candidate_found| L
    M["Admin Commands<br/>/reload /sync_commands"] --> N["Hot-reload cogs<br/>no restart"]

docs.title: Design Principles
docs.text:
1. Immutability: All context/response objects are frozen dataclasses
  2. Causation tracking: Event bus links parent→child relationships
  3. Isolation: Middleware failures don't crash the chain
  4. Throttling: Progress tracker batches Discord edits (default 1.5s)
  5. Lazy loading: Config values fetched on-demand for hot-reload support
  6. Observer pattern: Cogs register actions at startup, dispatcher invokes
  7. Error recovery: Graceful degradation for Discord API failures

docs.title: Knowledge Base & RAG System
docs.text:
11 min read · Last updated April 04, 2026 · 2,189 words

FORGE's knowledge base implements Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG) to ground Claude's responses in real documents. The system combines three retrieval mechanisms: ChromaDB vector search for semantic matching, Knowledge Graph for entity relationships, and Knowledge Router for query-aware retrieval strategy selection.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
The knowledge base system consists of:

  1. Vector Store (ChromaDB) — semantic document search
  2. Knowledge Graph — entity and relationship extraction
  3. Knowledge Router — intelligent query routing
  4. Memory Systems — user facts, reflections, conversation history
  5. SQLite Database — persistent storage for all features

Queries against the knowledge base are automatically routed to the optimal retrieval mechanism based on query intent, then results are injected into Claude's system prompt.

docs.title: Architecture Diagram
docs.text:
User Query
    ↓
[Knowledge Router] — Detect query type (entity / conceptual / hybrid)
    ↓
    ├─→ "Who is..." → [Knowledge Graph] (entity-first, + light vector)
    ├─→ "How to..." → [ChromaDB] (conceptual, vector-only)
    └─→ Default → [Graph + ChromaDB] (parallel, hybrid)
    ↓
[Merge Results] → System Prompt Context
    ↓
Claude Response

docs.title: Vector Store: ChromaDB
docs.text: Location: data/chroma_db/ Module: modules/knowledge_base.py

docs.title: Features
docs.text:
• Local, GPU-free: Uses ONNX embeddings (no GPU required)
  • Lazy-load: Defers initialization to first use, avoiding 30s boot spike
  • Auto-ingest: Watches knowledge/, jds/, and vault/ directories
  • File tracking: SQLite table kb_documents tracks ingestion state

docs.title: Supported File Types
docs.text:
• PDF, DOCX, TXT, MD, CSV, JSON
  • Job descriptions (from jds/ directory)
  • Vault notes (from vault/daily, vault/notes, vault/meetings, etc.)

docs.title: Document Chunking
docs.text:
CHUNK_SIZE = 500          # tokens (~2000 chars)
CHUNK_OVERLAP = 50        # tokens (~200 chars)
CHARS_PER_TOKEN = 4       # rough estimate

Text is split into overlapping chunks to preserve context boundaries. Overlap prevents losing information at chunk edges.

docs.title: Semantic Search
docs.text:
Score Threshold: 0.3 (cosine similarity)

Results with scores above 0.3 are returned. The threshold balances precision (fewer false positives) with recall (finding relevant content).

async def search(query: str, top_k: int = 5) -> list[dict]:
    """Search ChromaDB and return top K results with scores ≥ 0.3"""
    results = _collection.query(
        query_texts=[query],
        n_results=top_k * 2  # Fetch 2x, filter by threshold
    )
    # Filter: score >= 0.3
    filtered = [r for r in results if r['score'] >= 0.3]
    return filtered[:top_k]

docs.title: Lazy Loading
docs.text:
ChromaDB is not loaded on bot startup. Instead, it's initialized on first use (_ensure_chromadb()), avoiding: - 30-second CPU spike during boot - Unnecessary loading if KB is unused - Complex async state management at startup

When initialized, the system ingests all tracked files (JDs, knowledge/, vault/) in parallel.

docs.title: Knowledge Graph
docs.text: Location: modules/knowledge_graph.py

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Extracts and stores entities (people, organizations, projects, technologies) and relationships between them. Enables structured fact retrieval and entity-focused queries.

docs.title: Entity Types
docs.text:
VALID_ENTITY_TYPES = {
    "person", "place", "project", "organization", 
    "technology", "event"
}

docs.title: Relationships
docs.text:
VALID_PREDICATES = {
    "married_to", "parent_of", "works_at", "uses", "owns",
    "built_with", "related_to", "member_of", ...
}

docs.title: Database Tables
docs.text: Table Purpose entities Entity definitions (name, type, canonical form) relationships Links between entities (subject, predicate, object) entity_access Tracks which entities are most queried

docs.title: Entity Canonicalization
docs.text:
Entity names are normalized to prevent duplicates: - Lowercase - Strip whitespace - Replace - with _ - Remove '

Example: "John Smith" → "john smith", "John-Smith" → "john smith" (same entity)

docs.title: Caching
docs.text:
Entity profiles are cached with a 10-minute TTL: 

_entity_cache: dict[str, tuple[float, object]] = {}
_CACHE_TTL = 600  # seconds

Prevents repeated database queries for frequently accessed entities.

docs.title: Knowledge Router
docs.text: Location: modules/knowledge_router.py

docs.title: Query Classification
docs.text: Routes queries to the optimal retrieval strategy:

docs.title: Entity Queries → Knowledge Graph + Light Vector
docs.text:
Triggers on entity-focused prefixes: - "Who is...", "Where is...", "What is..." - "Tell me about...", "Search for...", "Info on..."

Behavior: Fetch from knowledge graph first, supplement with 2 vector results.

docs.title: Conceptual Queries → Vector Search Only
docs.text:
Triggers on explanatory prefixes and keywords: - "How to...", "Why does...", "Explain..." - "Compare...", "What are the differences...", "Best practice"

Behavior: Use ChromaDB only (no graph lookup).

docs.title: Mixed/Default → Hybrid (Parallel)
docs.text:
Triggers when no specific signals match.

Behavior: Query both graph and vector in parallel, merge results.

docs.title: Routing Logic
docs.text:
def route_query(query: str) -> str:
    """Returns: "entity", "conceptual", or "hybrid" """

    # 1. Check prefix matches (highest confidence)
    if query.lower().startswith(("who ", "where ", ...)):
        return "entity"

    if query.lower().startswith(("how ", "why ", ...)):
        return "conceptual"

    # 2. Check keywords anywhere in query
    if re.search(r"\b(how does|explain|trade-?offs?)\b", query):
        return "conceptual"

    # 3. Check entity patterns (proper nouns, @mentions)
    if re.search(r"[A-Z][a-z]+(?:\s+[A-Z][a-z]+)+", query):
        return "entity"

    return "hybrid"

docs.title: Memory Systems
docs.text: FORGE implements four complementary memory layers, each with distinct semantics and uses.

docs.title: 1. Conversation Memory (Short-Term)
docs.text:
Table: conversation_messages Module: modules/memory.py

Stores every message in a channel. When retrieving context, the system fetches: 1. Summaries (if older than context window allows) 2. Recent N messages (most recent first) 3. Current message

Use: Building multi-turn conversation context for Claude.

docs.title: 2. Forge Memory (Semantic, Category-Driven)
docs.text:
Table: None (file-based) File: ~/.claude/projects/C--Users-User-FORGE/memory/MEMORY.md Module: modules/forge_memory.py

A semantic, hierarchical knowledge system organized into categories:

Category Priority Auto-Decayed? identity 1 No (protected) family 2 No (protected) preference 3 Yes project 4 Yes decision 5 Yes person 6 Yes learning 7 Yes history 8 Yes

Use: Long-term semantic knowledge about Zack, projects, decisions, and learnings. Injected into system prompt in priority order.

Immutable Core (WORM_PERSONA): A write-once block describing FORGE's identity and capabilities, never updated.

docs.title: 3. Fact Memory (Per-User, Extracted)
docs.text:
Table: user_facts Module: modules/fact_memory.py

Automatically extracts structured facts about users via Claude Haiku after each conversation.

Data Model: 

@dataclass(frozen=True)
class UserFact:
    id: str                    # UUID
    user_id: str               # Discord user ID
    content: str               # "User prefers dark mode"
    category: str              # preference|knowledge|context|behavior|goal
    confidence: float          # 0.0-1.0
    created_at: str            # ISO 8601
    source: str                # "discord_msg_123"

Extraction Rules: - Confidence ≥ 0.9: explicit facts ("I live in LA") - Confidence 0.7-0.9: strongly implied facts - Confidence < 0.7: skipped

Deduplication: UNIQUE(user_id, content) prevents duplicate facts.

Use: Personalized responses tailored to individual user preferences, knowledge, and goals.

docs.title: 4. Reflection Store (Therapy-Like Introspection)
docs.text:
Tables: reflection_entries, reflection_sessions, therapist_memory Module: modules/reflection_store.py

Structured reflection data for introspection and pattern analysis.

Tables:

Table Purpose Key Fields reflection_entries Individual Q&A entries date, session, question, answer, category reflection_sessions Session grouping date, status (pending/completed), questions_json, answers_json, therapist_response therapist_memory Extracted patterns date, note, category (observation/pattern/insight)

Queries: - Find all reflection entries by date or category - Retrieve session progress (pending answers, completed sessions) - Extract patterns from therapist notes

Use: Self-reflection, pattern recognition, and personal growth tracking.

docs.title: Database Layer
docs.text: Location: core/database.py Path: data/forge.db

docs.title: Connection Management
docs.text:
async def get_db() -> aiosqlite.Connection:
    """Get or create shared database with health check."""
    # Reuses single connection, health-checks on access
    # Reconnects if connection stale

Features: - Single shared connection (all features use same DB) - Health check on every access (reconnect if stale) - WAL mode for concurrent access - 5-second busy timeout

docs.title: Key Configuration
docs.text:
_db.row_factory = aiosqlite.Row    # Dict-like row access
await _db.execute("PRAGMA journal_mode=WAL")      # Better concurrency
await _db.execute("PRAGMA busy_timeout=5000")     # 5s busy timeout

docs.title: Table Summary
docs.text: Table Purpose Key Columns kb_documents KB file tracking file_name, file_hash, chunk_count, ingested_at entities Knowledge graph nodes name, entity_type, canonical_name, access_count relationships Knowledge graph edges subject_id, predicate, object_id, valid_from, valid_to conversation_messages Channel message history channel_id, role, content, timestamp conversation_summaries Compressed older messages channel_id, summary, messages_covered user_facts Per-user extracted facts user_id, content, category, confidence reflection_entries Introspection Q&A date, session, question, answer, category reflection_sessions Reflection sessions date, status, questions_json, answers_json therapist_memory Pattern notes date, note, category

docs.title: API Endpoints (Cogs)
docs.text: Location: cogs/kb_actions.py

docs.title: Commands
docs.text: Command Action Handler kb_search <query> Search ChromaDB kb_search() → top_k=5 results kb_add <text> Add text to KB ingest_text() kb_add <path> Add file to KB ingest_file() (PDF/DOCX/TXT/MD/CSV) kb_list List ingested files list_documents() kb_stats Show KB size/file count get_kb_stats() kb_delete <filename> Remove file from KB delete_document()

docs.title: Search Result Format
docs.text:
📚 **Knowledge Base Search** — "<query>"

1. filename.md (87% match)
   [First 300 chars of chunk...]

2. other_file.pdf (72% match)
   [First 300 chars...]

💡 Say **send me that** or **send me #1** to get the source file.

docs.title: File Ingestion Flow
docs.text:
async def _ingest_if_new(fpath: Path, source: str = "knowledge"):
    """
    1. Compute file hash
    2. Check if already ingested (hash lookup)
    3. Extract text (PDF, DOCX, TXT, MD, CSV)
    4. Split into chunks (overlap: 50 tokens)
    5. Embed chunks via ChromaDB
    6. Track in kb_documents table
    """

docs.title: Text Extraction
docs.text:
• PDF: PyPDF2
  • DOCX: python-docx
  • TXT/MD: Raw text
  • CSV: Concatenate rows
  • JSON: Serialize values

docs.title: Multi-Source Ingestion
docs.text:
Startup ingest scans: - jds/ — All .txt job descriptions - knowledge/ — All supported file types - vault/daily/, vault/notes/, vault/meetings/, etc. — All .md markdown

Ingestion happens in parallel (4-semaphore) to avoid blocking.

docs.title: System Prompt Integration
docs.text: When Claude is invoked: 1. Route query (if applicable) → entity/conceptual/hybrid 2. Retrieve context from selected source(s) 3. Filter by score threshold (≥0.3) 4. Inject into system prompt as contextual knowledge 5. Claude responds with grounded, sourced information

docs.title: Example System Prompt Injection
docs.text:
<knowledge_context>
## Relevant Documents

**Document**: deployment-guide.md (92% match)
To deploy FORGE, run: docker compose -f docker-compose.prod.yml up -d

**Related**: config-reference.md (78% match)
Configure via environment variables in .env...

**Entity Context**: 
FORGE is a Discord bot by Zack. Uses Claude Haiku for classification...
</knowledge_context>

docs.title: Knowledge Base Cache
docs.text:
_kb_cache: dict[str, tuple[str, float]] = {}
_KB_CACHE_TTL = 300          # 5 minutes
_KB_CACHE_MAX = 50           # Max entries

Caches full search results by query hash. Prevents repeated ChromaDB queries for same query within 5 minutes.

docs.title: Entity Profile Cache
docs.text:
_entity_cache: dict[str, tuple[float, object]] = {}
_CACHE_TTL = 600  # 10 minutes

Caches entity profiles (with relationships) by entity ID.

docs.title: Auto-Prune
docs.text: Both caches auto-prune when: - Cache exceeds size limit (50 for KB, 200 for entities) - Entry TTL expires

docs.title: Key Files Reference
docs.text: File Purpose Key Functions modules/knowledge_base.py RAG retrieval, document ingestion search(), ingest_file(), ingest_text() modules/knowledge_graph.py Entity & relationship storage find_or_create_entity(), get_entity_profile() modules/knowledge_router.py Query routing route_query(), get_routed_context() modules/memory.py Conversation history save_message(), get_context_messages() modules/forge_memory.py Semantic user knowledge MEMORY.md file management modules/fact_memory.py Per-user fact extraction extract_facts(), save_fact() modules/reflection_store.py Introspection persistence save_reflection_session(), get_patterns() cogs/kb_actions.py User commands (kb_search, kb_add, etc.) handle_action() core/database.py SQLite connection & schema get_db(), init_tables()

docs.title: Why Multiple Memory Systems?
docs.text:
• Conversation Memory: Short-term, turn-by-turn context (can be summarized)
  • Forge Memory: Semantic, long-term, user-level knowledge (Zack-specific facts)
  • Fact Memory: Automatically extracted, per-user behavioral insights
  • Reflection Store: Introspection & pattern analysis

Each addresses a distinct use case and retrieval pattern.

docs.title: Why Lazy-Load ChromaDB?
docs.text: Initializing ChromaDB takes ~30 seconds and loads pydantic compatibility patches. Deferring to first use: - Keeps bot startup fast - Only initializes if KB actually used - Still async-safe (concurrent calls wait on first init)

docs.title: Why Knowledge Router?
docs.text:
Different query types benefit from different retrieval: - Entity queries (who, where) → graph-first (structured, exact) - Conceptual queries (how, why) → vector-only (semantic, flexible) - Mixed/unknown → hybrid (cover both)

Router prevents wasting context on irrelevant retrievals.

docs.title: Why ChromaDB Over Alternatives?
docs.text:
• Local: No API calls, no rate limits
  • GPU-free: ONNX embeddings work on CPU
  • Persistent: Survives bot restart
  • Query-flexible: Supports various filtering/metadata options

docs.title: Related Systems
docs.text:
• cogs/brain_actions.py — Uses knowledge context for multi-turn responses
  • modules/sourcing_pipeline.py — Enriches candidate profiles with KB context
  • modules/reflection_system.py — Stores introspection in reflection_store
  • core/autonomy_loop.py — May query KB for autonomous task context

docs.title: Glossary
docs.text:
• RAG: Retrieval Augmented Generation — grounding LLM responses in retrieved documents
  • Semantic Search: Finding documents by meaning/relevance, not keyword matching
  • Chunking: Splitting documents into overlapping segments for embedding
  • Entity: A unique concept (person, organization, technology) tracked in the knowledge graph
  • Predicate: A relationship type (works_at, owns, uses) between entities
  • Canonical Form: Normalized version of a name for deduplication
  • TTL: Time-to-Live — how long a cached value remains valid
  • WAL: Write-Ahead Logging — SQLite mode for better concurrent access

docs.title: Meeting Intelligence and Notes System
docs.text: 8 min read · Last updated April 04, 2026 · 1,686 words

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: FORGE's meeting intelligence system automatically captures meeting audio, generates timestamped notes with speaker attribution, and produces structured analysis with action items grouped by owner. The system identifies speakers using voice profile matching and Claude-powered contextual analysis, enabling searchable meeting notes across all past conversations. It runs as a standalone process for reliability and integrates with Google Calendar for automatic activation.

docs.title: Architecture Overview
docs.text:
The meeting intelligence system consists of six major components working together:

graph TD
    A["FORGE Bot<br/>Routes /record-start<br/>Manages post-pipeline<br/>Stores context"] --> B["Control File IPC<br/>recorder_control.json"]
    B --> C["forge_recorder.py<br/>Standalone Process<br/>Ring buffer 300s<br/>WASAPI + mic capture<br/>Survives crashes"]
    C --> D["Audio Files<br/>system.wav<br/>mic.wav"]
    D --> E["Post-Recording Pipeline"]
    E --> F["1. Merge + compress"]
    F --> G["2. AssemblyAI transcribe"]
    G --> H["3. Extract embeddings<br/>Resemblyzer"]
    H --> I["4. Speaker resolution<br/>Claude"]
    I --> J["5. Analyze + action items"]
    J --> K["6. Store KB + Discord"]

docs.title: Control Flow: Inter-Process Communication
docs.text:
1. User command: /record-start "Meeting Title" in Discord
  2. Bot writes control file: data/recorder_control.json with {"action": "start", "meeting": "Meeting Title"}
  3. Standalone recorder polls the control file, sees start command
  4. Recorder launches capture threads, starts ring buffer
  5. Recorder writes lockfile: data/recordings/.active_recording.json with active PID
  6. User command: /record-stop
  7. Bot writes stop command to control file
  8. Recorder stops capture, writes result to data/recorder_result.json
  9. Bot reads result, launches transcription pipeline

This IPC design means the recorder can survive FORGE crashing—even if the bot dies, the recorder keeps capturing until /record-stop is called or 1 hour elapses (hard limit).

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
Voice fingerprinting uses Resemblyzer, a neural speaker embedding model that converts audio segments into 256-dimensional vectors representing unique speaker characteristics.

Process: 1. During transcription, AssemblyAI segments audio by speaker (Speaker A, Speaker B, etc.) 2. For each segment, extract a 3-5 second audio clip 3. Resample to 16kHz (Resemblyzer's expected sample rate) 4. Pass through VoiceEncoder model → 256-dim embedding vector 5. Compare new embedding against stored speaker profiles using cosine similarity 6. If similarity > 0.75 (threshold), match to known speaker; otherwise flag as new speaker

docs.title: Threshold and Tuning
docs.text:
• Similarity threshold: 0.75 (cosine distance)
  • Sample rate: 16kHz (Resemblyzer requirement)
  • Embedding dimension: 256
  • Model: Pre-trained speaker recognition encoder from SpeechBrain

The threshold was chosen to balance false positives (matching wrong speakers) against false negatives (missing known speakers). High-confidence matches (>0.85) are auto-accepted; borderline matches (0.75-0.85) are flagged for human review.

docs.title: Speaker Profile Database
docs.text:
Profiles are stored in forge.db in three related tables:

speaker_fingerprints (per-utterance): - speaker_name — name of known speaker - embedding — 256-dim vector (JSON) - utterance — text of confirmed utterance - confidence — human confidence (0.0-1.0)

speaker_behavioral_stats (aggregated): - speaker_name - filler_rate — fillers per 100 words (um, uh, like, etc.) - question_rate — % of utterances that are questions - avg_utterance_length — mean words per utterance - common_phrases — distinctive 2-3 word phrases - sample_utterances — 3 most recent confirmed utterances - sample_size — total confirmed utterances

calibration_sessions (per-meeting accuracy): - meeting_date - total_speakers — how many unique speakers detected - matched_speakers — how many had names resolved - accuracy_score — (matched / total)

docs.title: Active Learning
docs.text:
The fingerprint database improves over time:

  1. Calibration: After initial speaker resolution, prompt user to verify speakers in Discord
  2. Correction: User runs /fix-speaker "Speaker A" "John" → stores utterances as confirmed
  3. Behavioral profiling: System analyzes confirmed utterances to extract filler rate, vocabulary patterns
  4. Next meeting: New speakers are compared against behavioral profiles and embeddings together

This two-stage approach catches speakers even if their voice changes (cold, stress, etc.) by also matching speaking patterns.

docs.title: Auto-Notes
docs.text:
calendar_auto_record.py provides calendar-based meeting detection:

Config: Stored in data/meeting_auto_record.json: 

{
  "enabled": true,
  "meetings": [
    {"pattern": "Eng Sync", "expected_speakers": ["Zack", "Arda", "Hank"]},
    {"pattern": "all-hands", "expected_speakers": ["Zack", "Arda", "Linda", "Hank"]}
  ]
}

How it works: 1. Periodic job (runs every 5 min) checks Google Calendar for next 30 minutes of events 2. For each event, check if event title contains any configured pattern (case-insensitive substring match) 3. If matched and meeting hasn't already been recorded today, auto-start recorder 4. If expected_speakers is provided, pass count to AssemblyAI as hint for diarization

Opt-out: Recurring meetings are stored in data/meeting_recorded_events.json to avoid re-recording same meeting twice in one day.

docs.title: Speaker Resolution
docs.text: speaker_resolution.py maps Speaker A/B/C to real names using a three-stage process:

docs.title: Stage 1: Context Building
docs.text: Gather hints from: - Me: Current user (Zack), role, notes - Contacts: Known colleagues (Arda, Linda, Hank, etc.), roles, notes - Recurring meetings: Day-of-week patterns ("Eng Sync is always Monday with Zack, Arda, Hank") - Ambiguity hints: Common confusion pairs ("If there are only 2 speakers, Speaker B is likely Hank, not Arda")

docs.title: Stage 2: Behavioral Comparison
docs.text: For each detected speaker: 1. Extract voice embedding from sample utterances 2. Compare against stored speaker fingerprints using cosine similarity 3. If high confidence match (>0.85), propose name 4. Combine with behavioral stats (filler rate, question patterns)

docs.title: Stage 3: Claude Arbitration
docs.text:
Send to Claude: - Context hints (who's likely in this meeting) - Transcription samples (first 2-3 utterances per speaker) - Embedding distances (if fingerprints exist: "Speaker A is 0.88 similar to Hank, 0.71 to Arda") - Behavioral signals (Speaker B has 12% filler rate → matches Arda profile)

Claude synthesizes all signals and returns structured mapping: 

{
  "Speaker A": "Zack",
  "Speaker B": "Arda",
  "Speaker C": "?Unknown",
  "confidence": {"Speaker A": 0.95, "Speaker B": 0.82, "Speaker C": 0.0}
}

Unknown speakers are flagged for manual calibration.

docs.title: Post-Processing Pipeline
docs.text: After recording stops, the system runs a sequential pipeline:

docs.title: 1. Audio Merge & Compression
docs.text:
• Read system.wav (system output) and mic.wav (microphone)
  • Mix both into a single stereo WAV (left=system, right=mic)
  • Compress to MP3 (128kbps) to save space
  • Store as recordings/{date}_{meeting_title}.mp3

docs.title: 2. Transcription (AssemblyAI)
docs.text:
• Upload MP3 to AssemblyAI API
  • Request speaker diarization with speaker_labels=True
  • Optionally pass speaker count hint from calendar if known
  • Poll for completion (typically 1-3 minutes for 1-hour meeting)
  • Result includes speaker labels (Speaker A, Speaker B, etc.) and timestamps

docs.title: 3. Voice Fingerprinting
docs.text:
• For each unique speaker label, extract 5-second audio clip at midpoint of their longest utterance
  • Resample to 16kHz
  • Compute 256-dim embedding vector
  • Compare against stored profiles in speaker_fingerprints table
  • Output similarity scores for top 3 matches per speaker

docs.title: 4. Speaker Name Resolution
docs.text:
• Use speaker_resolution.py to map labels to names
  • Store mapping in metadata file: recordings/{meeting_id}_speakers.json
  • Flag any unknown speakers for human verification

docs.title: 5. Analysis & Action Items
docs.text:
• Replace generic Speaker A/B/C with resolved names in transcript
  • Send full transcript to Claude
  • Extract: TL;DR, action items (grouped by owner), decisions made
  • Store analysis in recordings/{meeting_id}_analysis.json

docs.title: 6. Knowledge Base Ingestion
docs.text:
• Add transcript + analysis to FORGE's vector KB (ChromaDB)
  • Chunk by speaker turns for precise retrieval
  • Index by date, attendees, keywords

docs.title: 7. Discord Output
docs.text:
• Post summary to #meeting-recordings (if configured)
  • Include: title, attendees, TL;DR, action items, link to full transcript

docs.title: Key Files
docs.text: File Path Purpose skills/meeting/__init__.py Package entry point, exports skill API (start_recording, stop_recording, fix_speaker, auto_record_add, etc.) skills/meeting/recorder.py Control IPC, lockfile management, post-recording pipeline orchestration skills/meeting/transcription.py AssemblyAI API calls, speaker diarization, transcription polling skills/meeting/speaker_resolution.py Claude-powered speaker name mapping, context builder, embedding similarity scoring skills/meeting/voice_fingerprint.py Resemblyzer embedding extraction, fingerprint database queries, behavioral profiling skills/meeting/audio.py Audio merge, compression, WAV-to-MP3 conversion (deprecated: now in forge_recorder.py) skills/meeting/analysis.py Claude meeting analysis, action item extraction, Discord formatting skills/meeting/calendar_auto_record.py Google Calendar polling, pattern matching, auto-start recorder on meeting detection skills/meeting/speaker_fingerprint.py Fingerprint database schema (SQLite), behavioral stats aggregation, confidence tracking skills/meeting/data_models.py Shared state, config loaders, constants, lockfile management forge_recorder.py Standalone process, ring buffer capture, WASAPI loopback, control file polling, crash recovery

docs.title: "Stuck Typing" Post-Pipeline Hang
docs.text:
Symptom: After transcription completes, speaker resolution appears to hang for 2-5 minutes. No errors logged. Claude calls seem blocked.

Root cause: Resemblyzer's VoiceEncoder model loads lazily (first time used). Loading the ~100MB model from disk on first embedding extraction blocks the event loop.

Workaround: Pre-load encoder in bootstrap.py by calling _get_encoder() once on bot startup. Or move embedding extraction to background thread pool.

Status: Identified, not yet fixed. Consider batching embedding computation or async subprocess call.

docs.title: Process Recovery (Historical) (Historical)
docs.text:
Previous issue (now fixed): Recorder thread crashed on WASAPI initialization → lockfile not cleared → next start attempt read stale PID → think recording already active.

Fix applied: - Moved audio processing to standalone forge_recorder.py with independent lifecycle - Ring buffer survives FORGE crashes - Supervisor process auto-restarts recorder if it dies - Lockfile includes timestamp + PID validation

docs.title: AssemblyAI Speaker Count Hints
docs.text:
Limitation: Passing expected_speakers count to AssemblyAI improves diarization accuracy only if count is exact. If you hint 3 speakers but there are actually 2, AssemblyAI may hallucinate a third speaker with ~30% silence.

Mitigation: Use expected_speakers only when you're confident (calendar shows exact attendee count, or recurring meeting with fixed attendees).

docs.title: Voice Similarity on Noisy Calls
docs.text:
Known issue: On calls with high background noise or poor connection, Resemblyzer embeddings can drift, causing similarity scores to drop below 0.75 even for known speakers.

Mitigation: - Fallback to behavioral profile matching (filler rate, question rate) - Manually verify speakers in Discord after each meeting (/fix-speaker "Speaker A" "John") - Calibration feedback improves fingerprints for future meetings

docs.title: Large Meeting Performance
docs.text:
Limitation: For meetings with 5+ speakers, speaker resolution becomes ambiguous. Claude's contextual hints help, but absent explicit "who's who" cues, accuracy degrades.

Recommendation: For large meetings, pre-configure speakers in meeting_speakers.json contacts list with detailed notes.

docs.title: Related Systems
docs.text:
• holy-grails — Some meeting notes feed AI grail workflows
  • knowledge-base — Transcripts indexed for semantic search
  • brain — Claude Sonnet handles speaker resolution and analysis
  • autonomy-loop — Night mode can trigger auto-notes for scheduled meetings

docs.title: Overnight Engine & Scheduling
docs.text:
13 min read · Last updated April 04, 2026 · 2,604 words

Overnight productivity engine for FORGE — makes idle VPS hours productive without burning Zack's interactive time.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
The Overnight Engine is a family of systems that execute deep work (research, profiling, maintenance) when FORGE is idle — typically 9 PM to 7 AM Pacific. Instead of wasting 10 idle hours per night, these systems:

  • Continue pipeline work — score unscoreds, enrich profiles with GitHub data
  • Build company intelligence — research profiles for target companies
  • Raid company rosters — discover candidates via LinkedIn from unscanned AI companies
  • Replenish sourcing lists — auto-discover new companies to raid
  • Dream & synthesize — connect unrelated knowledge, surface insights
  • Maintain systems — re-index knowledge base, prune duplicates, decay stale memories

Cost cap: $10/night (actual spend typically $3–6) Architecture: APScheduler (cron) + 3-model pipeline (Haiku → Opus → Sonnet) Runs on: VPS only (7 PM–7 AM PDT)

docs.title: 1. Overnight Engine (core/overnight_engine.py)
docs.text:
Orchestrator for all overnight work cycles.

Component Details Cycles 4 per night at 9:30 PM, 12:30 AM, 3:30 AM, 6:30 AM Pacific Schedule CronTrigger via APScheduler (UTC hours 5, 8, 11, 14 = PDT -7) Cost tracking Nightly cap $10 USD, resets each night Task priority Company raids > unscored candidates > GitHub enrichment > company intel > KB maintenance

Task types (in execution order):

  1. Company raids (priority 0)
  2. Blast through unscanned AI companies (LinkedIn profiles via Apify)
  3. ~50 companies per cycle, ~30s each

  4. When exhausted, triggers company replenishment

  5. Score unscoreds (priority 1)

  6. Find candidates evaluated today with score = 0

  7. Run PRISM scoring to populate scores

  8. GitHub enrichment (priority 2)

  9. High-scoring candidates (3+) with missing profile_data

  10. Query GitHub API for activity, repos, contributions

  11. Company intelligence (priority 3)

  12. Distinct companies from recent candidate evals (7-day window)

  13. Build company profiles for market research

  14. KB maintenance (priority 4)

  15. Re-index ChromaDB for stale embeddings
  16. Prune duplicate knowledge entries

Key methods: - run_cycle() — Main overnight loop, checks VPS instance, cost cap, resets nightly - _pick_task() — Query DB for eligible work, rank by priority, return next task - _execute_task(task) — Route to appropriate module (raider, enricher, etc.) - get_last_night_summary() — Return summary of completed work

State persistence: - data/overnight_state.json — Tracks nightly cost, completed tasks, timestamps - data/overnight.log — Logs all cycles, task selection, cost tracking

docs.title: 2. Autonomy Loop (core/autonomy_loop.py)
docs.text:
Self-improvement engine — FORGE detects bugs/gaps and fixes itself via a 3-model pipeline.

Model Role Cost Haiku Research & root cause analysis ~$0.01–0.02 per cycle Opus Architectural design & risk assessment ~$0.10–0.20 per cycle Sonnet Implementation via copilot Copilot cost (tool_use)

5-phase workflow:

  1. ANALYZE (Local)
  2. Read friction signals from data/friction.jsonl
  3. Mine failure logs from forge.db (errors, timeouts)

  4. Identify capability gaps from chat history

  5. RESEARCH (Haiku, cheap)

  6. Investigate root cause
  7. Read relevant source files (up to 100 lines each)

  8. Suggest approach

  9. DESIGN (Opus, deep)

  10. Review Haiku's findings
  11. Propose architectural changes
  12. Score confidence (0.0–1.0)

  13. Skip if confidence < 0.4

  14. EXECUTE (Sonnet + Copilot)

  15. Follow Opus's plan
  16. Write code, run tests

  17. Commit with detailed message

  18. TRACK

  19. Log result to autonomy_log.jsonl
  20. Update autonomy_state.json (improvements count, cost, last_cycle)

Budgets: - MAX_IMPROVEMENTS_PER_DAY = 5 (env: AUTONOMY_MAX_DAILY) - DAILY_BUDGET_USD = 5.0 (env: AUTONOMY_DAILY_BUDGET) - CYCLE_INTERVAL_HOURS = 4 (env: AUTONOMY_CYCLE_HOURS)

Runs: Every 4–6 hours, async loop in bot.py

Dataclasses: - ImprovementCandidate — source, category, title, description, priority_score, evidence, estimated_complexity - ResearchReport — issue, root_cause, relevant_files, code_context, suggested_approach, cost_usd - DesignPlan — files_to_modify, changes_summary, risks, rollback_plan, confidence, cost_usd - ImprovementResult — candidate_title, status (success/failed/skipped/deferred), copilot_response, timestamp, cost_usd

docs.title: 3. Dream Mode (core/dream_mode.py)
docs.text:
Subconscious synthesis engine — connects unrelated knowledge, surfaces unexpected insights.

Aspect Details When Midnight–6 AM Pacific (off-hours) Frequency Max 3 dreams per night Cost cap $0.50/night Model Haiku (fast, cheap)

Process: 1. Gather context fragments: - Recent meeting action items - Recent candidates scored (5 most recent) - User facts (5 most recent) - Goals + milestones from KB

  1. Pass to _DREAM_SYSTEM prompt:
  2. Look for non-obvious patterns across domains
  3. Find skills/people from one area helping another
  4. Detect timing coincidences

  5. Flag contradictions needing resolution

  6. Synthesize → DreamInsight:

  7. title, synthesis (2–3 sentences), connections (pairs), confidence (0.0–1.0)

  8. Only store if confidence ≥ 0.5

  9. Store in dream_insights table:

  10. title, synthesis, connections (JSON array), confidence, dreamed_at, cost_usd, surfaced (flag)

Dataclass: 

@dataclass(frozen=True)
class DreamInsight:
    title: str
    synthesis: str
    connections: tuple  # ("fact A", "fact B"), ...
    confidence: float
    dreamed_at: str  # ISO timestamp
    cost_usd: float

Log: data/dream_log.jsonl

docs.title: 4. Dream Candidates (core/dream_candidates.py)
docs.text:
24/7 autonomous channel discovery — finds new talent communities beyond explicitly configured channels.

Feature Details Discovery methods AI-guided search for new platforms, communities, events Target stacks ClickHouse, OLAP, data engineering, Rust, Go, distributed systems, real-time analytics, observability Known platforms GitHub, LinkedIn, StackOverflow, Ashby (skip to avoid duplication) Cost estimation Ranks discoveries by relevance + cost impact Auto-wiring Approved channels auto-integrate into sourcing pipeline

Dataclass: 

@dataclass(frozen=True)
class ChannelDiscovery:
    id: int
    platform: str  # "discord", "slack", "meetup", "github_org", etc.
    name: str
    url: str
    description: str
    estimated_reach: int  # members/followers
    estimated_cost: str  # "free", "$X/mo", "one-time $X"
    relevance: float  # 0.0–1.0
    status: str  # "pending", "approved", "rejected", "wired"
    discovered_at: str

Workflow: 1. Query channel_discoveries table for pending recommendations 2. Score by relevance × cost-benefit 3. Surface top N to Zack (via Discord or briefing) 4. Auto-wire approved channels into sourcing pipeline

docs.title: APScheduler Core (modules/scheduler.py)
docs.text:
Central task scheduler using APScheduler (asyncio).

Component Details Timezone America/Los_Angeles (PST/PDT) Persistence SQLite scheduled_tasks table Task types Reminder (one-time), Recurring (cron), Special (briefing, raiding) Job defaults misfire_grace_time=3600, coalesce=True (skip missed occurrences)

Initialization: 

async def init_scheduler(bot, brain):
    # Load all enabled tasks from DB
    # Register each as APScheduler job (DateTrigger or CronTrigger)
    # Auto-clear browser cookies hourly
    # Catch up on missed recurring jobs (e.g., bot restart during scheduled task)

DB Schema (scheduled_tasks): - id, description, task_type, cron_expression, run_at (ISO), enabled, created_at, last_run

Key behaviors: - Cleanup past reminders — Disable one-time tasks with run_at in the past - Catch-up missed jobs — If bot restarts during a scheduled task, run it immediately - Auto-cookie-clear — Every hour, clear cookies from paywalled sites (bypass paywall limits)

Task execution: - Defers to _execute_task(task) per task_type - Checks is_my_scheduler_window() — only this FORGE instance runs tasks (prevents duplicates) - Logs result to DB (last_run timestamp)

docs.title: Predictive Scheduling (core/predictive_scheduling.py)
docs.text:
Learn user behavior patterns, schedule tasks intelligently.

Component Details Input Message timestamps from chat history (mining when user is active) Pattern detection Day-of-week, hour-of-day histograms, frequency, consistency score Output Optimal timing for reminders, deferred tasks during busy windows Tables user_action_log, detected_patterns, suggestion_responses

Pattern types: - Recurring behaviors (e.g., "Zack always checks messages 8–10 AM") - Busy windows (suppress reminders, defer expensive tasks) - Response patterns (when does Zack respond fastest to specific message types?)

Key method: 

async def should_send_now(
    importance: str,  # "low", "medium", "high"
    module: str       # "sourcing", "meeting", "maintenance"
) -> bool:

Checks current hour against detected patterns — returns True if timing is good.

docs.title: Sleep Worker (modules/sleep_worker.py)
docs.text:
Runs maintenance tasks when Discord channel is idle (> 120 seconds).

Component Details Idle threshold 120 seconds without incoming messages Check interval Every 30 seconds Once-per-idle rule Runs once, then waits for new activity before re-running Tasks Memory decay, KB re-indexing, cache cleanup

Usage: 

# In on_ready()
from modules.sleep_worker import start_sleep_worker, note_activity

await start_sleep_worker(brain, bot, scheduler)

# In on_message()
note_activity()  # Reset idle timer

Tasks executed (sequential): 1. Memory decay — reduce confidence on stale memories 2. KB cleanup — prune old, low-confidence entries 3. Cache eviction — clear old cached API responses 4. (More can be added)

docs.title: Company Raider (modules/company_raider.py)
docs.text:
End-to-end company roster scraping — blast through target companies, discover candidates.

Stage Details Input Company name (e.g., "Resolve AI") Search Apify LinkedIn actor (batch: 3–5yr + 6–10yr experience) Filter Confirm match, 2–7yr experience, Bay Area location Dedup Against Ashby pipeline, candidate_evals, profile_scrape_cache Store ALL profiles (8+ yr → senior_backlog tag) Output Qualified candidates ready for PRISM scoring

Key constraint: - ALWAYS batch Apify calls — 2 calls per company (3–5yr, 6–10yr), never 1 profile at a time - Previous violations cost $200+ in wasted Apify credits

Engineering keywords: engineer, developer, MTS, ML, AI, software, SWE, backend, full stack, data scientist, etc.

Bay Area filter: 50+ US cities + "Bay Area", "SF Bay", "San Francisco Bay", etc.

Blocklist: HockeyStack, Stripe, big tech (Google, Meta, Apple, Microsoft, etc.)

Usage: 

from modules.company_raider import raid, print_raid_results

results = await raid("Resolve AI", max_profiles=50)
print(print_raid_results(results))

docs.title: Company Replenisher (modules/company_replenisher.py)
docs.text:
Auto-discover new companies to raid — mine previous employers, search web for new AI startups.

Strategy Source Frequency Candidate mining Extract companies from profiles scored ≥ 3.0 Every cycle Web discovery Search YC batches, funding announcements, AI startup rankings Every cycle or weekly

Data source: data/ai_companies_master.csv

Blocklist (→ never add): - HockeyStack, all big tech (Google, Meta, Amazon, Microsoft, Apple, etc.) - Big finance & consulting (JPMorgan, Goldman Sachs, Deloitte, etc.) - Big enterprise (Salesforce, Adobe, VMware, SAP, etc.) - Research labs (UC Berkeley, Stanford, MIT, etc.) - Junk artifacts (Stealth Mode, Full-Time, Self-Employed, etc.)

Usage: 

from modules.company_replenisher import replenish_from_candidates, replenish_from_web

added = await replenish_from_candidates(min_score=3.0)  # Mine DB
added = await replenish_from_web()  # Web search

docs.title: News Aggregation (modules/news.py)
docs.text:
RSS-based news feed — polls 20+ sources every 30 minutes, posts to 📰-news channel.

Source Type Frequency Washington Post (5 feeds), Reuters, AP, CNN, BBC, Guardian, NPR, Intercept, ProPublica, Wired, Ars Technica Mainstream + investigative Every 30 min

Deduplication: SQLite seen_items table (7-day retention)

Rich embeds: Category emoji, source color coding, article summary (Claude Haiku)

Max posts per poll: 5 (avoid flooding)

docs.title: Key Files & Paths
docs.text:
File Purpose Key Tables core/overnight_engine.py Orchestrator for overnight cycles N/A (state → JSON) core/autonomy_loop.py Self-improvement via Haiku→Opus→Sonnet autonomy_log.jsonl, autonomy_state.json core/dream_mode.py Subconscious synthesis dream_insights (SQLite), dream_log.jsonl core/dream_candidates.py Auto-discover talent channels channel_discoveries (SQLite) modules/scheduler.py APScheduler orchestration scheduled_tasks (SQLite) core/predictive_scheduling.py User behavior pattern mining user_action_log, detected_patterns modules/sleep_worker.py Idle-triggered maintenance N/A modules/company_raider.py Company roster scraping candidate_evals, profile_scrape_cache modules/company_replenisher.py New company discovery ai_companies_master.csv modules/news.py RSS news aggregation N/A (Discord only)

State files: - data/overnight_state.json — Nightly cost, tasks, timestamps - data/autonomy_state.json — Improvements count, cost, last_cycle - data/dream_log.jsonl — Dream cycle history - data/autonomy_log.jsonl — Improvement attempts log

docs.title: Overnight Engine ($10/night cap)
docs.text: Task Typical cost Frequency Company raid (50 companies) $1.50–2.50 Every cycle Score unscoreds (batch of 10) $0.10–0.20 1× per cycle GitHub enrichment (batch of 5) $0.05–0.10 1× per cycle Company intel research $0.50–1.00 1× per cycle KB maintenance <$0.01 1× per cycle Total per cycle $2–4 4 cycles/night Total per night $8–16 Capped at $10

docs.title: Autonomy Loop ($5/day cap)

docs.text: Task Cost Gather context (DB queries) <$0.001 Haiku synthesis (3 dreams max) $0.10–0.15 per dream 3 dreams/night $0.30–0.45
